question: You're working as an agile project manager and the executive manager who will own the product would like to focus primarily on only set processes and tools to manage the project. What would you recommend to them instead of set processes and tools?

options.A: Focus on the definition of success as defined by the project manager
options.B: Focus primarily on the individuals and interactions involved
options.C: Focus primarily on the scope of the project
options.D: Focus primarily on the schedule of the project

explanation: The Agile Manifesto recommends focusing on individuals and interactions over processes and tools. Projects are run by people, products are developed by people for people to use, and interactions either make or break a project.

question: The executive management in your organization has assembled a new team and dictated that they will use agile for their product development. As the project progresses there is confusion about product scope and the definition of what done looks like. As an agile project manager, what will you recommend to the executive team?

options.A: Select a person from each part of the organization to assist to ensure diversity
options.B: Have the functional manager select their friends or their favorite team members
options.C: Encourage a self-organizing team to form including the people who asked for the requirements in the first place and the outcomes
options.D: Hire external consultants to run the project and create the product

explanation: Agile encourages self-organizing teams composed of the people who created the requirements and had the desired outcome in the first place. This gives a higher level of ownership and product expertise that cannot be bought.

question: You're running an agile project and you meet with the team for the next sprint planning meeting. What will you do next?

options.A: Negotiate the scope and costs of the project with the project sponsor
options.B: Walk through the three questions with the team: what did I do yesterday, what will I do today, and is there anything blocking me
options.C: The development team demonstrates the working feature to the product owner who marks it as complete
options.D: The product owner shares the updated backlog and the team discusses it to ensure a shared understanding

explanation: The product owner should share the updated backlog items in prioritized order and the entire team discusses them to ensure a shared understanding of what will be developed during the next sprint.

question: You're a project manager working on an agile project and you want your team to maximize the value they bring to the customer. What can you eliminate in order to maximize project value?

options.A: Daily stand-ups
options.B: Unnecessary features
options.C: Sprint reviews
options.D: Testing

explanation: Value can be maximized by eliminating unnecessary features, partially done work, delays, extra processes, task switching, waiting, defects, rework, and handoffs — all of which are considered waste in agile.

question: You're working on a project and the team have recently moved to an agile way of work. One of the team members is confused about scrum and what typically happens after the sprint review. What will you tell them happens next?

options.A: Hold a sprint retrospective with the team
options.B: Perform a backlog refinement with the product owner
options.C: Perform a story card elaboration with the three amigos (business/product owner, developers, and testers)
options.D: Hold a daily scrum

explanation: A sprint retrospective is held with the whole team after the sprint review but before the next sprint planning meeting. It is used to gather lessons learned and look for opportunities for improvement so the team continuously gets better.

question: You're working in an agile team and preparing the agile team charter. You work with the agile team to ensure the team charter answers four questions: why are we doing this project, and who benefits from the project. What are the other two questions the team charter should answer?

options.A: When will the project end, and what do we do with the product
options.B: What does done mean for the project, and how are we going to work together
options.C: How much risk is on the project, and what is the project scope
options.D: How many defects are allowed in the product, and how many testers are there

explanation: According to the Agile Practice Guide, the agile team charter typically covers why we are doing the project, who benefits, what does done look like, and how are we going to work together.

question: You're working with a new agile project manager and explain that agile favors value-based measurements instead of predictive-based measurements on a project. What is an example of value-based measurements?

options.A: Earned value management
options.B: Schedule performance index
options.C: Feature burn down chart, features delivered, and customer satisfaction
options.D: Variance at completion and cost performance index

explanation: Agile works with value delivered as its primary measurement. All other options are predictive measurements from traditional project management methodologies.

question: Agile Unified Process is an auxiliary agile method that focuses on performing more iterative cycles across seven key disciplines, incorporating associated feedback before formal delivery. What is NOT one of the seven key disciplines within a release for Agile Unified Process?

options.A: Discipline of the high level model
options.B: Discipline in managing stakeholders' expectations
options.C: Discipline in testing
options.D: Discipline in configuration management

explanation: Agile focuses on transparency to ensure customer needs are met, not specifically on managing customer expectations. According to the Agile Practice Guide, managing stakeholders' expectations is not one of the seven key disciplines of Agile Unified Process.

question: You're leading a coaching session with your project team on the additional agile and lean frameworks that have contributed to the agile way of work. What frameworks will you share with the team?

options.A: Project management plan and project charter
options.B: Scaled Agile Framework and Large-Scale Scrum
options.C: Working group meetings, post-mortem project meetings
options.D: Flow charting and affinity diagrams

explanation: Agile and lean frameworks include the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe), Large-Scale Scrum (LeSS), Scrum of Scrums (SoS), Dynamic Systems Development Method (DSDM), Kanban, Extreme Programming (XP), and Scrum, as noted in the Agile Practice Guide.

question: Introduced by Alistair Cockburn in his book Crystal Clear and created by IBM in 1991, this framework focuses on individuals and their interactions opposed to processes and tools. It is not a set process but a guideline for the team to collaborate and communicate. Which of the following is NOT one of the core beliefs of Crystal?

options.A: Technologies change techniques
options.B: People change functions
options.C: Cultures change norms
options.D: Distances change communication

explanation: Crystal Clear describes the three core beliefs as technologies changing techniques, cultures changing norms, and distances changing communication. These are reasons behind core agile practices such as stand-ups, collaborative user story creation, retrospectives, and the whole team approach.

question: You're working on a project in the role of Scrum Master and have set a meeting with the team and the product owner to refine the product backlog. What should the team do in this meeting?

options.A: Estimate and refine the work items
options.B: Prioritize the work items
options.C: Add features to the product backlog
options.D: Identify fixes

explanation: The product owner adjusts the priority of the product backlog items before the meeting; the team is responsible for estimating and refining those work items. The team needs to estimate as a team and figure out how much can be completed during a sprint, refining estimates with higher levels of detail each time.

question: You're working with a team who would like to become agile. The functional manager asks you what the difference between Kanban and Scrum is. What will you tell them?

options.A: Kanban teams plan their work in sprints or iterations
options.B: There are no work-in-progress limits in Kanban
options.C: Kanban teams work on a project as a whole
options.D: Kanban teams employ a pull system

explanation: The main difference between Scrum and Kanban is that Kanban teams employ a pull system — when an item of work is completed it triggers someone to pull the next item into the queue onto the board. Kanban teams work off a Kanban board that displays each task and limits how much work in progress can be in place.

question: You're working on a new project with an agile product development team who have self-formed and chosen a Scrum Master called Jeremy. Jeremy immediately sets a meeting with the team to explain and flesh out their team charter. What has made Jeremy a good choice for Scrum Master?

options.A: He likes to work within set processes
options.B: He has been with the company for more than five years
options.C: He shows servant leadership and engages the team in team decisions
options.D: He sticks to the plan and does not change

explanation: Jeremy shows the traits of servant leadership by engaging the team in team decisions, starting with the team charter and ground rules so everyone knows where they stand. By communicating the reasons for his decisions he is a suitable Scrum Master — less focused on command and control, increasing engagement and team output.

question: You're managing a project as an agile project manager and a business representative for the product you're delivering asks you how many defects were found in the latest round of testing. You don't know the answer off the top of your head. What should you do?

options.A: Guess the number of defects and then change the subject
options.B: Tell the business partner you will send an email with the information
options.C: Take him to the team area where the information radiator with project information is displayed
options.D: Ask the business partner to call the Scrum Master to get the correct answer

explanation: A core component of agile is visual management and transparency through the information radiator, which is in a common co-located place that anyone can see at any time. It includes team velocity, the Kanban board, product backlog, burn-down chart, latest test results, issues, and retrospective results.

question: You're working in a company that is moving from a waterfall methodology to agile. You mention to the business unit manager that the team will be better prepared to respond to change and meet customer needs using agile. Why is this?

options.A: The agile team works directly with a business owner or representative to constantly prioritize delivered value
options.B: The agile team delivers the product all in one go
options.C: The agile team is for developers only so development will improve
options.D: The business partners review statuses and ask for recommendations

explanation: Working with business partners or a customer representative almost daily is a key part of agile — the team hears what the business would like the results to be. Meeting face to face is better than reading requirements like emails or documents, providing much more information and enabling constant reprioritization of delivered value.

question: For agile life cycles two kinds of planning occur: release planning and iteration planning. In release planning, business representatives such as the product owner establish and prioritize the user stories for release in collaboration with the team, refining larger user stories into smaller ones. What is the INCORRECT description below regarding iteration planning and backlog preparation?

options.A: The backlog is the ordered list of all of the work presented in story form for a team
options.B: The coach encourages the team to work alone so no one bothers them
options.C: The facilitator encourages the team to work in triads of developer, tester, and product owner or business analyst
options.D: The triad discuss, write, and then place enough stories into an iteration and enough features for a first release

explanation: Answers A, C, and D are correct descriptions of backlog preparation for iteration planning according to the Agile Practice Guide from the Agile Alliance and PMI. Option B is incorrect because agile emphasizes continuous collaboration as a team, not working alone in isolation.

question: Behaviour Driven Development (BDD) allows a developer to focus on testing code based on the expected behavior of the software and is a method of writing user stories and acceptance criteria. What is the correct description regarding Behaviour Driven Development acceptance-based criteria?

options.A: Make, buy, and do
options.B: Tell, show, and repeat
options.C: Find, create, and perform
options.D: Given, when, then

explanation: Given, when, then is how Behaviour Driven Development is used — particularly when outlining user story cards for iteration. It highlights: given a certain situation, when something happens, then we want this to happen.

question: Every project has characteristics around requirements, delivery, change, and goals. Understanding the different types of life cycle allows you to choose the right one for your project. What is the correct description for a waterfall approach?

options.A: Predictive, fixed requirements, performed once for the entire project, in a single delivery
options.B: Predictive, variable requirements, performed once for the entire project, in a single delivery
options.C: Predictive, fixed requirements, performed multiple times, in multiple deliveries
options.D: Predictive, dynamic requirements, repeated until correct, in a single delivery

explanation: A predictive or waterfall project management life cycle focuses on fixed requirements performed once for the project in a single delivery. Agile, by contrast, continually releases features and delivers value in smaller, more frequent increments.

question: Every project has characteristics around requirements, delivery, change, and goals. Understanding the different types of life cycle allows you to choose the right one for the circumstances of your project. What is the correct description for an iterative project approach?

options.A: Fixed requirements, repeated until correct, in multiple deliveries
options.B: Dynamic requirements, repeated until correct, in a single delivery
options.C: Dynamic requirements, performed once for a given increment, in frequent smaller deliveries
options.D: Fixed requirements, performed once for the project, in a single delivery

explanation: Iterative methods have dynamic requirements that are ever-changing and ever-improving — repeated with feedback until correct — but they are delivered only once (a single delivery). This contrasts with incremental approaches which have multiple smaller deliveries.

question: The team charter is also a social contract. Which of the below is NOT usually a part of an agile team charter?

options.A: Team values such as sustainable pace and core hours
options.B: Working agreements such as what ready means and what done means
options.C: Ground rules such as one person talking in a meeting
options.D: Schedule performance index and cost performance index for the project

explanation: The agile team charter focuses on the vision, the why, and the team's way of work. The SPI and CPI (cost performance index) are earned value management metrics from a predictive/waterfall project methodology and are not part of a traditional agile team charter.

question:
The team charter is also a social contract. Which of the below is NOT usually a part of an agile team charter?
A) Team values such as sustainable pace and core hours
B) Working agreements such as what 'ready' means and what 'done' means
C) Ground rules such as one person talking in a meeting
D) Schedule performance index and cost performance index for the project

options.A: Team values such as sustainable pace and core hours
options.B: Working agreements such as what 'ready' means and what 'done' means
options.C: Ground rules such as one person talking in a meeting
options.D: Schedule performance index and cost performance index for the project

explanation: The agile team charter focuses on the vision, the 'why', and the team's way of work. SPI and CPI are earned value management metrics from a predictive (waterfall) project methodology and are not part of a traditional agile team charter.

question:
You're working with a team that is new to agile and you're using Scrum to manage the project. The business representative is confused about the difference between a sprint review and a sprint retrospective. What will you tell them?
A) Sprint reviews demonstrate the work completed in the sprint; sprint retrospectives look at all the work completed in the project
B) Sprint reviews discuss what is worked on in the sprint; sprint retrospectives are done at the end of the project for a lessons learned opportunity
C) Sprint reviews review the lessons learned; sprint retrospectives look at the history of the product retrospectively
D) Sprint reviews are for product demonstrations; sprint retrospectives are for lessons learned

options.A: Sprint reviews demonstrate the work completed in the sprint; sprint retrospectives look at all the work completed in the project
options.B: Sprint reviews discuss what is worked on in the sprint; sprint retrospectives are done at the end of the project for a lessons learned opportunity
options.C: Sprint reviews review the lessons learned; sprint retrospectives look at the history of the product retrospectively
options.D: Sprint reviews are for product demonstrations; sprint retrospectives are for lessons learned

explanation: Sprint reviews are for product demonstrations to the business or customer, while sprint retrospectives are for lessons learned (what went well, what didn't, what did I learn), ideally performed at the end of each iteration to continuously improve the team's process.

question:
The agile team for which you're the project manager has determined several key features to deliver to the customer. How should these tasks be tackled by the team?
A) The features should be prioritized, tested, and delivered incrementally
B) The features should be delivered quickly whether or not they're complete to gather feedback
C) The features should be delivered according to the project plan
D) All the features should be delivered at the end of the project

options.A: The features should be prioritized, tested, and delivered incrementally
options.B: The features should be delivered quickly whether or not they're complete to gather feedback
options.C: The features should be delivered according to the project plan
options.D: All the features should be delivered at the end of the project

explanation: Agile is a combination of incremental delivery and iterative development, so business value is continuously and iteratively improved and prioritized. Features should be complete, tested, and quality is everyone's responsibility — not just delivered fast but delivered with quality built in.

question:
You're working on an agile project and the project team have just finished an iteration. All tasks for the iteration have been completed and you're ready to move on to the next iteration, but you're not sure what the team should work on. What will you do next?
A) Follow the project plan developed early in the project
B) Follow the project management office who has already determined how the project should progress
C) Ask the product owner who continuously prioritizes the backlog
D) Ask the project coach or team leader to determine the next iteration

options.A: Follow the project plan developed early in the project
options.B: Follow the project management office who has already determined how the project should progress
options.C: Ask the product owner who continuously prioritizes the backlog
options.D: Ask the project coach or team leader to determine the next iteration

explanation: The product owner represents the customer and continuously prioritizes the backlog of features. The team works through the list of items that have identifiable value and have been prioritized by the customer, ensuring business value is always being maximized.

question:
While meeting with the customers or business owners, they introduce several pieces of new functionality into the product backlog. The deadline for the product cannot be changed. What will you do next?
A) Tell the team to work overtime in order to deliver the project
B) Stand by your team and tell the customers the project will not be delivered on time
C) Be sure the customers understand that lower priority features may drop off the project completely
D) Agree with the customers to have all new items put into a separate category that will be completed if time allows

options.A: Tell the team to work overtime in order to deliver the project
options.B: Stand by your team and tell the customers the project will not be delivered on time
options.C: Be sure the customers understand that lower priority features may drop off the project completely
options.D: Agree with the customers to have all new items put into a separate category that will be completed if time allows

explanation: An agile project can accept changes even late in development. While cost, quality, and schedule are often fixed in agile, scope can vary over time — some lower priority features may drop off while others become prioritized above them. A maintainable pace is also required so project managers must not overwork the team.

question:
Every project has characteristics around requirements, delivery, change, and goals. Understanding the different types of lifecycle will allow you to choose the right one for the circumstances of your project. What is the correct description for an incremental project management approach?
A) Fixed requirements, activities performed once, in multiple deliveries
B) Dynamic requirements, activity is repeated until correct, in one large delivery
C) Dynamic requirements, performed once for a given increment, with frequent smaller deliveries
D) Fixed requirements, performed once, infrequent smaller deliveries

options.A: Fixed requirements, activities performed once, in multiple deliveries
options.B: Dynamic requirements, activity is repeated until correct, in one large delivery
options.C: Dynamic requirements, performed once for a given increment, with frequent smaller deliveries
options.D: Fixed requirements, performed once, infrequent smaller deliveries

explanation: An incremental project management approach includes dynamic requirements performed once for each increment, with those increments delivered in frequent smaller deliveries. When combined with iterative improvement this forms the foundation of an agile approach.

question:
There are certain core practices that are common amongst various agile frameworks and are used within any team that calls themselves agile. Which of the below is NOT a core agile practice?
A) Team iteration planning
B) Collaborative user story creation between the business, customer, developers, and testers
C) Demonstrations and reviews of the features
D) Autocratic leadership to ensure the work gets done

options.A: Team iteration planning
options.B: Collaborative user story creation between the business, customer, developers, and testers
options.C: Demonstrations and reviews of the features
options.D: Autocratic leadership to ensure the work gets done

explanation: The preferred style of leadership in an agile team is servant leadership, not autocratic leadership. Autocratic leadership is a one-person top-down affair, while servant leadership builds psychological safety by serving and growing the team so everyone can engage and contribute. All other items listed are core agile practices.

question:
Poor specifications are often a major reason for project failure. In agile development, user stories are written with developers, testers, and business representatives with frequent reviews to ensure they are correct. Which of the following is NOT correct regarding collaborative user story creation?
A) A user story addresses both functional and non-functional requirements
B) The project sponsor must sign off on all user stories in advance
C) A user story includes acceptance criteria for the feature
D) Characteristics for acceptance criteria must be estimable, small, and testable

options.A: A user story addresses both functional and non-functional requirements
options.B: The project sponsor must sign off on all user stories in advance
options.C: A user story includes acceptance criteria for the feature
options.D: Characteristics for acceptance criteria must be estimable, small, and testable

explanation: While it is common for developers, testers, and business representatives to agree on the story card criteria, it is not signed off by the project sponsor directly. The product owner, representing the business or customer, grooms the feature backlog to ensure the highest value items are always being delivered.

question:
In continuous integration, configuration management, compilation, software build, deployment, and testing are wrapped into a single automated repeatable process. Which of the following is correct regarding continuous integration?
A) Defects in the code are not an issue, making it an ideal method
B) Defects are detected more quickly because teams constantly integrate, build, and test their work
C) Defects are integrated into the work as part of test-driven development
D) Defects are collected and fixed in an iteration at the end of the project to avoid delay

options.A: Defects in the code are not an issue, making it an ideal method
options.B: Defects are detected more quickly because teams constantly integrate, build, and test their work
options.C: Defects are integrated into the work as part of test-driven development
options.D: Defects are collected and fixed in an iteration at the end of the project to avoid delay

explanation: Continuous integration merges code to a test environment using an automated process including automated regression tests to ensure nothing is broken, which allows the team to find and fix defects more quickly. Test-driven development (writing a test first that fails, then writing code to make it pass) is a separate but related practice.

question:
Agile has certain core practices used within any team working in an agile way. Which of the following is NOT a core practice of agile?
A) The whole team approach where any person who is needed is brought into the project team
B) Early and frequent feedback
C) Daily stand-ups to report progress and raise blockers
D) Reporting to executives to ensure they're happy with your project

options.A: The whole team approach where any person who is needed is brought into the project team
options.B: Early and frequent feedback
options.C: Daily stand-ups to report progress and raise blockers
options.D: Reporting to executives to ensure they're happy with your project

explanation: Reporting to executives is not a direct agile core practice. In agile, information is made visible and accessible to anyone who needs it — through burn-down charts, product backlogs, and demonstrations or reviews — rather than through formal executive reporting. All other items listed are agile core practices.

question: You're working on an agile project as an agile project manager. You look at the product backlog and ensure that several features or minimum viable products (MVPs) can be scheduled for delivery throughout the duration of the project. Why is that a good idea?

options.A: To show executives that progress is being made by the development team
options.B: To keep the project duration short
options.C: To enable users to test the product for quality and give feedback
options.D: To enable the business to get value quickly as the features are delivered

explanation: Minimum viable products are usable features in their own right and they are delivered incrementally, allowing business value to be gained more quickly. This also enables gathering feedback on whether the features are having the desired results.

question: You're working on a complex software project but you see that the team uses a whiteboard and writes tasks on cards or sticky notes. The business representative seems put off by the use of these low-tech tools on such a high-profile technology project. What will you tell them?

options.A: The team are using low-tech tools so that low-tech people can understand them
options.B: A whiteboard is actually a complex thing — they just don't know it yet
options.C: The team is using the principle of visual management and by using a physical board it is transparent to anyone who needs that information
options.D: The team will drop everything and develop a new software-based kanban board to avoid embarrassment

explanation: Visual management is a lean principle borrowed into agile where key pieces of information are made as accessible as possible, often found in the team area. It means anyone can walk through that area and see what they need at a glance, and a kanban board is an example of a low-tech high-touch tool.

question: Agile teams practice frequent verification and validation during and at the end of each iteration through demonstrations and reviews. Which of the following is NOT a benefit of demos and reviews?

options.A: It enables the team to catch mistakes and mismatched customer expectations early
options.B: It keeps everyone busy and gives them something to do
options.C: It is easy to manage since there are multiple feedback loops
options.D: It reduces cost overruns as mistakes are found early prior to additional tasks being built on a faulty foundation

explanation: Busy work does not add value to the project and is not a benefit of agile demos and reviews. The actual benefits include catching mistakes early, reducing cost overruns, and creating multiple feedback loops that help deliver value.

question: You're on an agile software implementation project with a very short deadline. How do agile teams shorten the time between identifying a defect and resolving that defect?

options.A: Continuous integration
options.B: Daily build and smoke tests
options.C: Usability testing
options.D: Exploration testing

explanation: Continuous integration aims to merge the code and have a working build once a day, then executes automated tests quickly to see if anything is broken. This frequent checking ensures less time passes before a problem is identified.

question: With all the different methods of testing either a software or a product deliverable, when should the team determine the acceptance test criteria for the feature or story card?

options.A: When the first iteration is complete
options.B: During the estimation and elaboration of the card with the product owner, business analysts, and development team
options.C: Before the product or feature is prioritized
options.D: At the end of the project

explanation: Acceptance tests and criteria are given before the development of a deliverable and during elaboration, often in a given/when/then format (behavior-driven development). We need acceptance criteria before development starts so that the team can deliver to those criteria.

question: Every project has characteristics around requirements, delivery, change, and goals. Understanding the different types of life cycle will allow you to choose the right one for the circumstances of your project. Choose the correct description for an agile approach.

options.A: Dynamic requirements, repeated until correct, with frequent smaller deliveries
options.B: Dynamic requirements, repeated until correct, in one large single delivery
options.C: Fixed requirements, activities performed once, with infrequent small deliveries
options.D: Fixed requirements, activities performed once, in one large delivery

explanation: The agile project management approach includes dynamic requirements repeated until correct with frequent smaller deliveries, as stated in the Agile Practice Guide from the Project Management Institute and the Agile Alliance.

question: Continuous integration wraps software build, deployment, and testing into a single automated repeatable process. Which of the following best describes what this merging process does?

options.A: It separates each feature into its own development pool
options.B: It ensures no code is changed
options.C: It integrates all changed components regularly, at least once a day
options.D: It enables new features for the product backlog

explanation: Continuous integration is designed to merge all changes made to the software and test them automatically, discovering any defects or issues on a daily basis. This is one of the fastest ways to find defects and ensure that everything works as a whole.

question: Crystal is one of the original frameworks that contributed to agile. It is designed to scale and realizes that each project may require a slightly tailored set of practices based on the size and complexity of the project. What are the core values of Crystal?

options.A: People, interaction, and community
options.B: Finding people, building people, and leading people
options.C: Building products, buying products, and selling products
options.D: Profit, persistence, and power

explanation: Crystal's core values include people, interaction, and community, in the same way that agile is based around individuals and interactions over processes and tools. Crystal was created by Alistair Cockburn in the early 1990s and was an input into agile.

question: Crystal is designed to scale and realizes that each project may require a slightly different tailored set of practices based on its size and complexity. What are some of the ways that Crystal scales?

options.A: Crystal blue, indigo, and violet based on number of features
options.B: Crystal clear, yellow, orange, and red based on the number of people
options.C: Crystal metal, wood, and water based on the flow of story cards through the kanban board
options.D: Crystal high, low, and medium based on dollars spent on the project

explanation: Crystal Clear, Yellow, Orange, and Red are based on factors such as the number of people involved (zero to eight and beyond), money involved, and the general comfort level of the project often involving risk. These scaling methodologies help agile teams apply the right practices for their project size.

question: You're working through an agile project with your team meeting daily in a daily stand-up. What is NOT one of the reasons that agile teams use daily stand-ups?

options.A: To micro-commit to the team
options.B: To uncover and remove blockers to ensure work flows freely through the team
options.C: To ensure work flows freely through the team
options.D: To report to the executive team

explanation: Stand-ups are used to micro-commit to each other, uncover blockers, and ensure the work flows freely through the team. While everyone including an executive is welcome to join, stand-ups are not specifically for reporting to an executive team.

question: You're a project manager on an agile project that is halfway through the project. The project sponsor is starting to raise concerns that the project will not be completed in the allotted time. What will you do to gather the information necessary to answer the project sponsor to see how the project is actually going?

options.A: Review the kanban board and the current work in progress
options.B: Check the product backlog for remaining features
options.C: Review the team velocity, the product burn down chart, and other key performance indicators
options.D: Have a team meeting and log everyone's status

explanation: Agile key performance indicators include team velocity (rate of progress in story points) and remaining work shown on a burn down chart. Reviewing these KPIs gives the most reliable picture of how the project is tracking against the allotted time.

question: You're attending a kickoff meeting with the agile team and the business owners. The scrum master recommends using MoSCoW to help prioritize the work with the product and the business owners. How does MoSCoW prioritize the product backlog?

options.A: Using priority one, two, three and four
options.B: Using low, medium, high
options.C: Using must have, should have, could have, and will not have
options.D: Using multiple dots that people can assign to features they like

explanation: The MoSCoW method of prioritization labels tasks as must have, should have, could have, and will not have (or would like to have). Other prioritization techniques include multi-voting with dots, monopoly money, kano analysis, and fist of five.

question: Agile teams use different methods to manage their work in progress or WIP. The business sponsor believes that removing the limits to work in progress will help the team take on more work and get their product delivered faster. What will you tell the business sponsor?

options.A: Remove the work in progress limits to push as much work through as possible
options.B: Dictate over time and no holidays for the team to get things done
options.C: Work in progress limits ensure the team focuses on one main thing at a time and avoids the time cost of switching tasks
options.D: Work in progress can be seen on the kanban board so it doesn't matter what the limit is

explanation: In agile, work is taken on based on team velocity. The kanban board shows work in progress and who is working on what. Research has proven that multitasking or switching tasks reduces team effectiveness as the team gets up to speed again, so WIP limits protect the team from over-commitment.

question: You're working on an agile team that includes many stakeholders who have not worked with an agile project in the past. Several stakeholders have some valid concerns about some agile methods. As a servant leader or project coach, what will you do to make them more comfortable with the process?

options.A: Educate the stakeholders, address their concerns, and keep them engaged
options.B: Tell them that it's worked in the past so it will work here too
options.C: Dictate to the stakeholders that this is how all future projects must be executed
options.D: Go back to a waterfall style project management method

explanation: A servant leader includes the team in decisions and grows the team through education and coaching. Those convinced against their will are of the same opinion still, so dictating or simply asserting past success is less effective than genuine coaching and engagement.

question: You're working on an agile project with stakeholders who are keen to know why it is so important to incorporate multiple touch points with them before, during, and after iterations.

options.A: To raise and manage changes and to identify potential risks and issues
options.B: To inform stakeholders of the development team's direction
options.C: To ensure executive business executives hear about the team's value with multiple meetings
options.D: To have them invest more time in the project so they are more committed

explanation: Frequent touch points in agile ensure that business features are reviewed regularly, meet stakeholder expectations, and that risks are addressed throughout development. Stand-ups, demonstrations, reviews, and retrospectives all allow changes to be raised early and quality to be everyone's responsibility.

question: Teams use daily stand-ups to micro commit to each other, uncover and remove blockers, and ensure that work flows smoothly through the team. What is NOT true about stand-ups?

options.A: They're often time boxed to 15 minutes
options.B: The team walks through the kanban or the task board
options.C: The team facilitator or anyone in the team can facilitate
options.D: Only people in the project team can attend

explanation: Agile teams are open and transparent and encourage stakeholders to pull information and work when needed — anyone can attend, learn, watch, or contribute. Stand-ups are time boxed to 15 minutes, the team walks through the kanban board, and any team member can and should be encouraged to facilitate.

question: Features are completed in an agile project usually in the form of multiple user stories. When a feature is complete, the team periodically demonstrates the working product to the customer. When do these demonstrations or reviews occur?

options.A: Only at the end of a project when the stakeholders are available
options.B: Often at the end of an iteration, or when enough features have been completed into a set that is coherent
options.C: Often at the start of a project
options.D: Often when the team wants to, as they are busy working

explanation: Agile sprint reviews or feature demonstrations occur at the end of an iteration or when enough cards or features have been completed into a set that can be showcased to the customer or product owner.

question: Disciplined Agile is a process decision framework that blends various agile techniques. Which of the following principles are NOT a part of Disciplined Agile?

options.A: People first — enumerating roles and organization elements at various levels
options.B: Learning oriented
options.C: Self-promotion — working to improve your own personal brand
options.D: Goal driven — tailoring processes to achieve specific outcomes

explanation: Agile works transparently for all stakeholders; self-promotion is not part of the agile way. Instead the team serves each other, ensures blockers are removed, and information is freely available to everyone inside or outside the team.

question: Disciplined Agile is a process decision framework that blends various agile techniques. Which of the following are principles FROM Disciplined Agile?

options.A: Learning oriented, goal driven, and scalable
options.B: Implementing, iterating, and integrating
options.C: Excellence, guidance, and cadence
options.D: Making, doing, and being

explanation: Learning oriented, goal driven, and scalable are all themes within agile in general and are key principles of the Disciplined Agile framework. These reflect the core agile values of continuous improvement, outcome focus, and the ability to grow across the organization.

question: Dynamic Systems Delivery Method (DSDM) is one of the founding methods with an input into agile, designed to add more rigor to the rising iterative methods of the 1990s. It's most known for its emphasis on constraint-driven delivery. Which of the following best describes this emphasis?

options.A: Focus on the business need and deliver on time — scope is variable and prioritized to meet fixed constraints
options.B: Add every feature and stop when done
options.C: Find all the defects or don't approve the release
options.D: Push fast delivery over quality so we can learn from our mistakes

explanation: DSDM focuses on the business need and delivers on time; scope is often variable while schedule is often set, meaning features are managed and prioritized with the product owner so the highest-value items are delivered first within the fixed time constraint.

question: You are working with the developers in an agile team who are using a test driven development approach. They ask you whether they should operate using something called a red green refactor. What are they referring to?

options.A: Where no code is developed during a red status, some code is developed during a green status, and new features are taken in during refactor
options.B: A traffic light approach to bringing in new features
options.C: Only releasing features when they are given the green light
options.D: Where a test is written first that fails, then it passes once the solution is developed, and then it can be refactored or streamlined

explanation: Red green refactor is the TDD cycle: a test is written first (red/fail), code is added until the test passes (green), then the code is refactored or streamlined. This is the core process of test driven development (TDD).

question: You are an agile project manager and you are working with the product owner of the agile team. There are several key deliverables that have been identified and listed in the product backlog. What will the agile team do next?

options.A: All deliverables should go live only at the end of the project
options.B: The tasks should be delivered but not tested to get value to the customer as quickly as possible
options.C: The tasks should be prioritized, developed and tested incrementally
options.D: Tasks should be planned carefully and delivered according to their plan

explanation: Agile develops and delivers in features or increments, meaning tasks are prioritized for each increment and tested for each increment as well. Quality testing is still important even when delivering quickly, as quality is everyone's responsibility.

question: A team new to agile has worked closely with the product owner and has identified the highest value features of the project. What is one reason to break down those features into increments and deliver them as soon as possible?

options.A: To get all the work out of the way so the team can relax
options.B: So the project life cycle can be shortened
options.C: So the product owner can report to her executive manager that work is being done
options.D: To reduce the risk of the feature no longer providing value for the customer

explanation: The longer a feature takes to deliver, the higher the risk that it will no longer provide the value it was intended to provide. Value driven delivery prioritizes features by highest value and delivers them quickly for maximum customer benefit.

question: You are an agile project manager working closely with the scrum master and the agile team. The scrum master mentions that agile works differently with risk compared to a traditional project. He sets a meeting with you and the team to talk through agile risks. What will you do next?

options.A: Ensure the team notes and schedules high risk activities early in the project so they are mitigated quickly
options.B: Ensure the team identifies risks, places them in a register, and mitigates them by the end of the project
options.C: Ensure the team keeps the risk register to itself so other stakeholders don't think less of the project
options.D: Ensure the risk is only captured around people because an agile team prefers individuals and interactions over processes and tools

explanation: An agile team delivers in increments and iterates towards improvement by prioritizing high risk features first, so that future risks for the project are reduced. Addressing high risk items early is a key principle of agile risk management.

question: You are a project manager on an agile project that requires an external third party to develop software for one of your features. You have worked with the project team to gather the requirements and the backlog of product features. You begin working with your procurement department on a contract for the vendor. What will you do next?

options.A: Request that the contract is one that accommodates changes to product scope
options.B: Go back to the business and ask them to deliver the feature
options.C: Raise a risk to the delivery of your project based on the external vendor
options.D: Find an existing time and materials contract from the organization's organizational process assets

explanation: Agile works with changing scope and cost as the product is completed incrementally, but primarily with a fixed schedule and quality. When working with procurement, the contract must accommodate those changing requirements so the agile way of work is not inhibited.

question: Dynamic systems delivery method (DSDM) is one of the founding methods with an input into agile, designed to add more rigor to the rising iterative methods of the 1990s. It is most known for its emphasis on constraint driven delivery, which sets cost, quality and time at the beginning and then uses formalized prioritization of scope to meet those constraints. What is DSDM most known for its emphasis on?

options.A: Constraint driven delivery, which ensures that scope is variable while cost, quality and time are set
options.B: Single delivery, which ensures everything is correct and approved before the project goes live
options.C: Stakeholders, which ensures that everyone is involved in gathering scope, quality and cost requirements
options.D: Cost, which ensures that the project closes once funds are depleted

explanation: Agile methods as we know them today work with a variable scope while cost, quality and schedule are often set — the opposite of a predictive waterfall method which sets scope up front. DSDM formalizes this through constraint driven delivery.

question: Agile projects have short iterations enabling the project team to receive early and continuous feedback on product quality throughout the development cycle. By getting frequent customer feedback as the project progresses, agile teams can incorporate most new changes into the product and the development process. What is NOT one of the benefits of this approach?

options.A: Avoiding requirements misunderstandings that may not have been detected until later in the development cycle
options.B: Clarifying customer feature requests, making them available for the customer to use early
options.C: Discovering, isolating and resolving quality problems early
options.D: Having one big release at the end of the project that everyone can celebrate

explanation: Agile methods work with multiple features releasing them in increments so the customer can use them and provide feedback. Having a single release at the end is a waterfall approach and is not a benefit of the agile iterative model.

question: Enterprise scrum is a framework designed to apply the scrum method at an organizational level, not just within a single product development effort. What is NOT a part of leadership within enterprise scrum?

options.A: Extending the use of scrum across all aspects of the organization
options.B: Role playing the role of scrum master before the first iteration
options.C: Generalizing the scrum techniques to apply easily at various levels
options.D: Scaling the scrum method with supplemental techniques as necessary

explanation: All answers are correct as enterprise scrum practices except B — role playing the role of scrum master before the first iteration is not a part of enterprise scrum. Enterprise scrum is all about scaling the scrum methodology to use it across teams as well as within teams.

question: You are an agile project manager tasked with evolving your part of the organization into agile. What is NOT something that you would do when making this change?

options.A: Treat the change as an agile project with its own backlog of changes that could be introduced by the team based on perceived value
options.B: Treat each of the changes as an experiment tested for a short period of time to determine suitability or the need for further refinement
options.C: Use kanban boards to track progress, showing new approaches in use as done and things to be tried as in progress
options.D: Direct the teams to take on agile in one go, moving ahead without wavering

explanation: Directing teams to take on agile all at once is opposite to the agile approach — it is more of a waterfall approach. Adopting agile is itself an incremental approach where changes are treated as experiments using a backlog and kanban board to track progress.

question: Extreme programming (XP) is a software development method based on frequent cycles. It is known for popularizing a holistic set of 12 primary practices. Which of the following does XP outline as a requirement?

options.A: To sit together in the whole team approach in an informative workplace
options.B: To write reports for the executive team
options.C: Rotating the roles so everyone gets experience in something they like
options.D: Developing all changes for a single release

explanation: Agile core practices from XP include the whole team approach and an informative workplace such as visual management using kanban, burn down charts and backlogs. Writing executive reports, rotating roles, and single releases are not primary XP practices.

question: You are an agile project manager working with the project sponsor in the beginning stages of an agile project. The project sponsor mentions that they would like your help creating the project charter. You tell them that you will also do a team charter for your agile team that will include the project sponsor. What does this mean?

options.A: You'll capture the team values, communication guidelines, and decision making process separately to the project purpose, requirements, and summary schedule.
options.B: You'll create a project plan with scope, schedule, cost and quality outlines to follow.
options.C: You will take over from the project sponsor and direct the team on future customer requirements.
options.D: You'll defer to the team for all queries in the way of a servant leader.

explanation: An agile project manager charters both the project and the team. A project charter focuses on the business case, project purpose, high level requirements, stakeholders, and risks; a team charter focuses on the agreements in how the team will operate and is created by the team for the team.

question: You're working with the team on the team charter and you've outlined the project vision, mission, and agreed meeting times and accepted team behaviour. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask the team to get to work straight away because agile teams move swiftly.
options.B: Send the team charter to the project sponsor for their sign off so the project can begin.
options.C: Agree on and include the definition of done for team features and tasks.
options.D: Set a meeting for two weeks time so you can continue to develop the charter iteratively.

explanation: The definition of done is an important part of the team charter to ensure the team knows when a task is completed, when they can pull the next tasks, and so everyone agrees what success looks like. The project sponsor should be included in the team charter but does not need to sign off on it.

question: You are working on an agile project gathering requirements from the business stakeholders with the product owner. During the discussion, different people seem to have different understandings of what the feature will look like. What will you do next?

options.A: Ask the stakeholders to elaborate their ideas better and set another meeting in a week's time.
options.B: Set another meeting in a week's time.
options.C: Draw a storyboard prototype of the requirement so all stakeholders can see it, adjust it on the fly, and agree.
options.D: Give your understanding of the feature and ask the stakeholders to agree or disagree.
options.E: Park the feature for now and begin work on a feature that is elaborated better.

explanation: Gaining consensus by developing a wireframe or storyboard will confirm everyone has a complete understanding of what the output is — it acts as an early increment that stakeholders can see, feel, and adjust in the moment, which aligns with agile's iterative and communication-focused approach.

question: You're working as a project manager with an agile project team. The project sponsor sits on another floor and prefers to communicate by email only. What will you do next?

options.A: Ensure that you communicate by email only with the project sponsor so they are comfortable.
options.B: Stop communicating with the project sponsor until they at least come to a few project meetings.
options.C: Ask the project sponsor to co-locate with the agile team for a few days a week to ensure face-to-face communication and access to the visual project information.
options.D: Set meetings with the project sponsor so you can update them personally.

explanation: An agile project team aims to work in a co-located space where ideas and information can flow freely. Face-to-face communication provides the best opportunity to ask questions, get immediate feedback, and understand body language, while kanban boards and other visual tools in the team area further support transparency.

question: As an agile project manager you understand the need to operate with openness and transparency with your team and with your stakeholders. How will you encourage knowledge sharing throughout the project?

options.A: Tell your team what to do during work hours so they can learn from you.
options.B: Write process documents for the team to read through in their spare time.
options.C: Make key information visible in the team area and ensure all impacted stakeholders are invited to the daily stand-ups, retrospectives, and even iteration planning.
options.D: Form a key group of executive stakeholders to share project information with and ensure your good reputation within the company.

explanation: An agile team makes project information visible and accessible to all. Agile teams also prefer people and interactions over processes and tools, as stated in the agile manifesto, so ceremonies like stand-ups, retrospectives, and iteration planning are the primary vehicles for knowledge sharing.

question: You've been working in an agile team for some time and an executive from the business area asks how the team has been able to deliver so consistently over the past year. You tell him you're using extreme programming (XP). What are some of the primary practices of XP that contributed to the team's success?

options.A: Real customer involvement, team continuity, and a sustainable pace.
options.B: Ensuring executive reports, testing continually, and raising defects.
options.C: Pivoting frequently, ensuring long meetings, and one final delivery.
options.D: Finding the right product, finding the right people, and finding the right price.

explanation: The extreme programming primary practices include real customer involvement, team continuity, and working at a sustainable pace. Agile encourages all of these through stand-ups, backlog grooming with the real customer or product owner, and planning to the team's velocity.

question: You're working with a seasoned agile team who are very familiar with extreme programming in their way of work. They walk through a set of principles that they frequently use. What is NOT one of the primary practices of XP?

options.A: Planning with user stories.
options.B: Test first programming.
options.C: Incremental design.
options.D: First in best rest.

explanation: Extreme programming works with 12 primary principles including planning with user stories, test first programming, and incremental design — all of which align with core agile principles. 'First in best rest' is not an agile or extreme programming principle.

question: You are testing the product being delivered by an agile team in increments. You notice that there have been no defects passed on to the end user. You also notice that you're working with an extreme programming methodology for the team. What are the XP practices that led to this result?

options.A: Regular refactoring and daily deployment of an integrated code base.
options.B: Having real users test the product when it's released.
options.C: Moving team members around to different roles for a shared experience.
options.D: Ensuring the team does exactly what the scrum master says.

explanation: Extreme programming works with continuous integration — deploying a single code base on a daily basis and running automated regression tests so defects are picked up quickly. Regular refactoring (simplifying and cleaning the code) also ensures it is easier to work with and fix defects.

question: You are an agile project manager working with an agile coach and planning the next sprint with the team. The agile coach advises you to build in some slack for the sprint. What does this mean?

options.A: To use the messaging service Slack to communicate as a team.
options.B: Allowing the best performers in the team to slack off as a reward for their hard work.
options.C: Adding a card for refactoring code that can be replaced if an emergency comes up.
options.D: Creating a burn down chart and a burn up chart together.

explanation: Building slack into a sprint means adding a refactoring card to the iteration that can be removed if an emergency arises. If no emergency occurs, the team uses that time to clean and streamline the code base, making it easier to work with in the future.

question: The team are planning their first upcoming sprint on an agile project. They have worked with a product owner who has gathered a backlog of features in prioritized order. As an agile project manager, what must you do to ensure you can plan future sprints correctly?

options.A: Find the best performer in the team so you know who you can rely on.
options.B: Find your team's velocity over the next few sprints so you can set a sustainable pace.
options.C: Set a working group meeting to gain executive buy-in.
options.D: Create the project management plan and draw out the schedule in advance.

explanation: An agile sprint is based on the team's velocity — the amount of points, cards, or features the team is able to complete on average during a regular sprint. You will need to complete at least one sprint to get an idea of the velocity, which then allows you to plan a sustainable pace for future sprints.

question: You are an agile project manager and your organization is moving away from a waterfall to an agile way of work. After reading the agile manifesto, the functional manager you are working with mentions 'individuals and interactions over processes and tools' and asks you how you will do it. What do you tell them?

options.A: Create ceremonies such as the daily stand up, iteration planning, demos and reviews, and retrospectives that encourage team interaction
options.B: Create a process for the team to review that shows the way of work
options.C: Ask the project sponsor to interact with the team by sharing some of their journey
options.D: Ensure that the tools the team have to work with are state-of-the-art software solutions

explanation: Agile ceremonies such as stand-ups, iteration planning, sprint reviews, and retrospectives are all designed to encourage individuals and interactions as a team. Agile does not require high-tech tools and can be run with a whiteboard or sticky notes.

question: You are an experienced functional manager who has been asked to lead an agile project team. Your executive manager is calling it the project coach or scrum master role. You know that agile leadership might be different to what you are used to. What sort of leadership style will you embody?

options.A: Servant leadership
options.B: Directive leadership
options.C: Visionary leadership
options.D: Laissez-faire leadership

explanation: An agile leader is primarily a servant leader — the focus is on removing blockers for the team, helping them problem-solve, and growing their skills. Directive, visionary, and laissez-faire styles do not reflect the collaborative and team-empowering approach required in agile.

question: You are working as an agile project manager in a new project team. Many project team members are despondent and do not show up for the meetings and ceremonies that you have set. What will you do next?

options.A: Raise a risk in the risk register around project goals and ask the project sponsor to mitigate the risk
options.B: Have a coaching session with the team, teaching the what and the why behind agile ceremonies, and ensure each meeting has a clear outcome, time and agenda
options.C: Cancel all meetings for now and write some documentation for the team on what you would like them to do
options.D: Make the decisions yourself and dictate the outcomes to the team

explanation: An agile leader focuses on coaching the team, removing blockers, and ensuring interaction. Teaching the purpose of key ceremonies (stand-ups, sprint reviews, retrospectives) and ensuring clear agendas helps re-engage the team. (Agile Practice Guide)

question: You are an experienced project manager who is used to a directive leadership style where people do exactly as you say. Now you are working in an agile team where that style of leadership is not accepted. What is one thing you can change to move to a more servant leadership style?

options.A: Ensure that the executive team makes all the decisions
options.B: Ensure that stakeholders collectively agree on and share decisions
options.C: Defer to the product owner and the business to make all the decisions
options.D: Find an extremely competent person in the project team and delegate all the decisions to them

explanation: Agile methods foster team empowerment through collective agreement and shared decision-making, where the team and stakeholders arrive at decisions together rather than one party directing. (Agile Practice Guide — team empowerment and decision making)

question: You are working on the requirements for the next iteration and a decision needs to be made by the team. The product owner suggests using a show of hands to indicate team members who are for or against the decision. As the agile project manager, you object to this approach. Why?

options.A: Agile is about software — the team should use software to vote for the decision
options.B: The decision may not go in your favor, so it is best just to do it yourself
options.C: Decisions should take time to work through and should not be voted on quickly
options.D: By making the decisions visible, team members may be influenced by others in the team, especially executives, without the benefit of discussion

explanation: With a visible show-of-hands vote there is no discussion, and team members are more easily influenced by others' decisions — especially those they report to outside the project. Methods like anonymous voting or fist-of-five allow concerns to be raised and discussed first. (Agile Practice Guide — decision making)

question: An agile team in your organization would like to add a few key agile processes to their way of work. The project manager mentions performing root cause analysis when problems appear on features, focusing on features that bring in the most revenue with pay per use, and daily stand-ups. What agile method is she most likely referring to?

options.A: Scrum
options.B: Extreme programming
options.C: Kanban
options.D: Iteration planning

explanation: Extreme programming (XP) has 14 secondary practices including root cause analysis, focusing on features that bring in the most revenue with pay per use, and shrinking teams through improvements over time. These are not practices of Scrum or Kanban. (XP secondary practices)

question: An agile team in your organization would like to include feature driven development practices into their way of work. You tell them that feature driven development activities are supported by a core set of software engineering best practices. What practices will the team start with?

options.A: Feature releases, feature celebrations and feature iterations
options.B: Developing by feature, feature teams and visibility of progress and results
options.C: Finding features, developing features, testing features
options.D: Ensuring executive features are allocated and developed

explanation: Feature Driven Development (FDD) focuses on core engineering best practices such as developing by feature, working in feature teams, inspections, regular builds, and visibility of progress. Working in feature teams allows members to become domain experts. (FDD core practices)

question: Your agile team has been working with feature driven development as a model for their agile way of work for some time and are ready to add more feature driven development practices to the things that they do. What can you do next as a team?

options.A: Ask the product owner for more features in the backlog
options.B: Hold a retrospective to gather ways to improve
options.C: Incorporate configuration management and individual class ownership
options.D: Send key representatives to the scrum of scrums across the organization

explanation: More advanced FDD core engineering practices include configuration management (ensuring changes are known and tracked) and individual class ownership (assigning parts of the code to a single owner who becomes the expert). These are specific to FDD and move the team deeper into the methodology. (FDD engineering practices)

question: An executive in your organization approaches you about working in an agile team. They have heard about feature driven development and would like to try it as a test and learn in your team. What do they mean by this?

options.A: Your team will rely on previous feature designs for your work
options.B: Your team features certain abilities and showcases them to other teams
options.C: Your team enjoys the feature of working with an agile organization
options.D: Your team will trial developing an overall model, then planning, designing and building by those features

explanation: Agile FDD starts with an overall model, then plans, designs and builds by those features. Once a feature is complete, the process can begin again, incorporating any learning from the previous feature — making it a true test-and-learn approach. (FDD — Agile Practice Guide)

question: For kanban, or flow-based agile, iterations follow the number of story cards in the work in progress (WIP) limit shown on the visual kanban board. The team pulls cards from the backlog column based on their capacity. These story cards provide an increment of value and are a what?

options.A: Primary driver of executive behavior
options.B: Primary measure of progress
options.C: Primary way to pay for the cost of an agile team
options.D: Primary enabler of people's engagement

explanation: The key to kanban is making progress visible — story cards on the kanban board are a primary measure of progress, and visible progress naturally improves team engagement. Blockers are clear for everyone to see, enabling the team to swarm around problems. (Kanban — Agile Practice Guide)

question: You have just come on board an agile team and a decision needs to be made about product requirements. The scrum master asks the team to show either thumbs up, thumbs down, or sideways. What does a thumb sideways mean?

options.A: The team member doesn't mind about the decision either way
options.B: The team member has a concern or conflict that needs further discussion
options.C: The team member defers to the executive sponsor to decide
options.D: The decision is outside the team member's expertise and they choose not to vote

explanation: In agile thumb voting, a sideways thumb signals that the team member has a concern or conflict with the decision and would like to discuss it further — the same principle applies to the fist of five technique. This keeps discussion open rather than forcing a silent for/against split.

question: You're at the beginning of a new iteration and about to plan and decide what gets worked on in the upcoming sprint. You decide to invite all project stakeholders to the planning meeting to decide what to work on next. Why is it important that all stakeholders are involved in decision making?

options.A: So you can keep an eye on what they're doing
options.B: Business representatives can report back to their managers on the project
options.C: The team feels important with lots of stakeholders present
options.D: Project stakeholders don't reject a decision that wasn't theirs

explanation: Giving ownership in decisions to the team and stakeholders is one way to gain their buy-in; if stakeholders are involved in decision-making they will be more committed to any decision made and to the project itself. Studies show people are more likely to reject ideas they had no ownership in.

question: You're on an agile team and notice that it is different to other project areas you have worked on. The project manager tells you there are certain ways to ensure that stakeholders stay engaged. What is he talking about?

options.A: Removing any team member who is not engaged in the project
options.B: Giving any extra work to the better-engaged team members
options.C: Ensuring visibility, transparency and progress of the work through charts and information in the common team area
options.D: Offering a possible promotion to the most engaged team member at the end of the project

explanation: Visible progress helps maintain and obtain team engagement and is facilitated through the use of the information radiator — the team area containing the kanban board, burn-down chart, and product backlog — making it clearly visible to everyone.

question: You've been identified as a stakeholder on an agile software development team. As you join the stand-up you're surprised that there are only eight people on the team and no more, even though the deliverable is quite large. Why is the team so small?

options.A: The agile methodology is being trialled as a cross-discipline self-forming team
options.B: One person was selected for each role necessary and no more
options.C: Agile methods recommend the delivery team to be 12 or fewer members

explanation: Agile methods recommend a team of 12 or fewer, typically between three and nine members; this keeps communication channels small and ensures information travels quickly so the team can do their work efficiently.

question: As the lead on an agile project you have carefully cultivated a team of T-shaped people. What is a T-shaped person?

options.A: A person that has flat knowledge up top and long knowledge down the bottom
options.B: A person with a wide range of general skills and one deep skill or knowledge area
options.C: A person with a wide range of leadership skills, as everyone should lead on an agile team
options.D: A person that has more contacts in the management layers and a few contacts in the team layer

explanation: A generalising specialist (T-shaped person) has one deep knowledge area or skill and a wide range of general interests or skills; an agile team is ideally made up of these generalising specialists who can do more than one thing but also do their core role well.

question: In 2001 a group of individuals representing the most widely used lightweight software development methodologies agreed on a common set of values and principles which became known as the Agile Manifesto. In that manifesto they valued what?

options.A: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
options.B: Executive reporting over bottom-up reporting
options.C: Scrum masters over project managers
options.D: Doing the work over viewing the work

explanation: The Agile Manifesto is made up of four core values, one of which is valuing individuals and interactions over processes and tools. This foundational value underpins how agile teams prioritise collaboration and communication.

question: You're planning the next iteration for your agile team as the agile lead. The team is going to select user stories from the prioritized release backlog, elaborate those user stories, and estimate the work needed for each user story. The number of stories selected is based on what?

options.A: What the scrum master says as they know best
options.B: The team's velocity, which is the rate at which a team can complete work
options.C: The feature driven development model
options.D: What the team wants to work on to ensure individuals work within their strengths

explanation: The team's velocity is the amount of work they complete on average in a given iteration, measured in points, cards, features, or any method the team agrees on; the number of stories selected for a sprint is based on the average velocity from previous iterations.

question: Your team is standing around a board with cards on it. Each person is answering these questions in a round-robin fashion: What did I complete since last time? What am I planning to complete between now and next time? Is anything blocking me? What is the team doing?

options.A: A luncheon loan
options.B: A working group meeting
options.C: The daily stand-up
options.D: A retrospective

explanation: The daily stand-up in an agile team asks in round-robin fashion what each member did, what they plan to do, and whether anything is blocking them; the team typically talks to the cards in flight on the kanban board to make progress visual.

question: There are multiple project life cycles you can choose from depending on the type of project environment, deliverable, and stakeholders. You're working on a project that requires features of an overall product to be delivered on a regular basis. What sort of project life cycle will you choose to work with?

options.A: Waterfall
options.B: Agile
options.C: Iterative
options.D: Incremental

explanation: An incremental project lifecycle specifically delivers features in increments of value over the life cycle of a project; while agile is a combination of iterative and incremental, the strict PMI definition of delivering regularly released features aligns with the incremental lifecycle.

question: You're working as an agile project manager in a new team that's not familiar with agile. The executives are not as comfortable using agile in full just yet. You suggest using a hybrid approach to begin with to help the organization meet their goals. What does a hybrid approach mean?

options.A: A tailored combination of predictive, iterative, incremental, and agile approaches
options.B: Using a project management plan to plan the project while delivering features
options.C: A combination of reactive and proactive approaches
options.D: Using agile only when it's needed, such as in a software development team

explanation: A hybrid project life cycle approach incorporates parts of predictive, iterative, incremental, and agile methodologies; it allows the project manager to tailor the approach to suit the situation, drawing on whichever elements best fit the project's needs.

question: The agile team leader works through servant leadership to the team and has built a safe environment for disagreement. How has this empowered the team to move forward without obstacles?

options.A: The team is able to create a list of reasons why tasks cannot be completed
options.B: The team has no conflict and they get along so well
options.C: The team leader takes responsibility for all decisions
options.D: The team is encouraged to participate in discussion and even disagreement

explanation: A safe environment for disagreement empowers teams by encouraging constructive discussion and disagreement to ensure risks and alternative ideas are found. This is only maintainable in a team with high psychological safety, and is a key aspect of servant leadership in agile.

question: New projects go through the Tuckman model of forming, storming, norming and performing. You've just taken the lead of this new project with a newly formed team and you're surprised to find little conflict and that the forming stage has gone so quickly. How did that happen? Which of the following might be the reason?

options.A: The team members have worked together on previous projects and are following the same agile ceremonies and methods
options.B: You've made all the project decisions yourself so they don't have to worry
