options.C: The team is in line for a promotion so they really want to get along
options.D: The team members really care about the product being delivered

explanation: If the team has already worked together, they have been through much of the forming and storming stages and may already be performing together. Using the same ceremonies and methods means they have already worked through what new project teams must navigate. This can help skip or shorten the early Tuckman stages.

question: You're a project manager of a new agile project. You ask questions of your team to elicit their needs and help them problem solve issues as they arise. What role are you fulfilling in an agile team?

options.A: Project director
options.B: Project coach
options.C: Project support
options.D: Project executive

explanation: The project coach role in agile centres around servant leadership — coaching the team, gathering feedback, helping them along their journey, and removing blockers. The agile lead or scrum master functions as a project coach who grows the team rather than directing them.

question: You're an agile project manager leading a new software project. During the last retrospective there was very little engagement and feedback and the team seems to be losing interest in their work. What will you do to improve the situation?

options.A: Hold another retrospective to try and extract more information
options.B: Align the project goals to each team member's personal goals
options.C: Hold a vote on whether to continue the project or not
options.D: Get executive management involved so team members can raise their engagement

explanation: Aligning project goals to each team member's personal goals is the most powerful approach to improve engagement. If a previous retrospective did not elicit information, holding another one would be doing the same thing but expecting a different result; aligning goals to individuals' strengths naturally helps people work harder and care more.

question: You're working as a project manager in a team that is moving to an agile way of work. You've previously engaged the team in a single weekly meeting, a working group meeting, with email as the primary communication. What changes will you make in the new agile team?

options.A: Ensure that reports are created weekly for executive management
options.B: Set up a teleconference with different parts of your project team
options.C: Co-locate the team where possible and focus on frequent face-to-face communication
options.D: Have frequent one-on-ones with your project team to get their point of view in private

explanation: Agile focuses on individuals and interaction — co-locating the team allows information to be gained by osmosis (overheard background conversations) while face-to-face discussion is the recommended communication method. This replaces slow email-based and weekly-meeting-based communication.

question: Kanban translates to 'visual sign' or 'card' in Japanese and is a form of visual management taken from lean manufacturing. Which of the following best describes the primary focus of kanban in an agile team?

options.A: Monitoring work in progress and enabling pull and flow
options.B: Enabling executive reporting
options.C: Work velocity and story card elaboration
options.D: Enabling team participation

explanation: Kanban is a framework from the Toyota Production System utilized in software and knowledge work; it focuses on the kanban board to show and limit the current work in progress, enabling pull and flow. Cards are pulled only when ready, and the flow of tasks through the board is monitored to remove blockers.

question: You're working with an agile team who use kanban to manage the flow of work through the team. The scrum master or agile lead mentions the concept of 'pull' which helps the team manage the work. What does this mean?

options.A: The team gathers around the team velocity board each morning to ensure the work is being done
options.B: The team pulls work only when they are ready instead of the work in progress constantly building up
options.C: The team works with a pull and push approach between developers and testers
options.D: The scrum master tells the team to pull the appropriate levers that enable the right features to be developed

explanation: Kanban allows team members to pull new work onto the kanban board only when they are ready, limiting the amount of work in progress and encouraging an optimal flow of work. If something is blocked the team can swarm around that problem and fix it.

question: You're walking through the next feature with the product owner in an agile team. He is new to agile and unsure of what kanban really is. He lists a few things for you to check. Which of the below is NOT one of the core properties of kanban?

options.A: Visualize the workflow and limit the work in progress
options.B: Manage flow and enable pull
options.C: Implement feedback loops and improve collaboratively
options.D: Follow procedures and don't deviate from the plan

explanation: Kanban is a method borrowed from the Toyota Production System/lean that focuses on limiting WIP, ensuring feedback loops are in place, and making work visible so blockages are clear. 'Follow procedures and don't deviate from the plan' is the opposite of the agile/kanban approach of continuous improvement.

question: You're working on an agile project as an agile project manager and have been using kanban to monitor the work and keep it visible for all to see. What is NOT a defining principle of kanban?

options.A: Start with the current state and respect the current process
options.B: Agree to pursue incremental evolutionary change
options.C: Lead at all levels
options.D: Directive leadership over wasteful team participation

explanation: Kanban, like agile itself, encourages team participation over directive leadership; it keeps work visible and makes blockers clear. Directive leadership is contrary to the servant leadership philosophy at the core of both kanban and agile.

question: In 2001 a group of individuals representing the most widely used lightweight software development methodologies agreed on a common set of practices, values and principles which became known as the Agile Manifesto. Which of the following is one of the manifesto's core values?

options.A: Directive leading over servant leadership
options.B: Working software over comprehensive documentation
options.C: Making useful large features over making small increments
options.D: Pushing work through quickly over limiting work in progress

explanation: One of the four core values of the Agile Manifesto is 'working software over comprehensive documentation' — releasing working features that customers can see, feel, and touch rather than writing manuals about them. Agile prefers releasing small increments of working software.

question: You've joined a new project team. You're moving to an agile way of work and part of this is co-locating the team to gain the benefit of osmotic communication. Your project sponsor asks what this means. What do you tell them?

options.A: The team is able to meet more frequently
options.B: It's easier to hand off work to someone if it meets their strengths
options.C: It's a written process made available to everyone in the team for transparency
options.D: The team will overhear conversations and information in the common area that will help them solve future problems

explanation: Osmotic communication refers to the accidental overhearing of background information that may later end up being important — team members absorb knowledge passively by being co-located. This is a key benefit of co-locating an agile team.

question: You're working in an agile team and you are not co-located. They are in various locations around the country. As the agile lead, how will you get the benefit of agile without a co-located team?

options.A: Utilize group messaging, daily teleconference stand-ups, electronic kanban and backlogs
options.B: Ask the organization to relocate the team
options.C: Hire or find new team members from within the organization who are in the same place
options.D: Remove all meetings so people can focus on their work without distraction

explanation: It may not be feasible to move entire teams or hire new ones; utilizing the many electronic options so the team can still get the benefit of frequent contact is the most cost-effective approach. Tools like group messaging, daily teleconference stand-ups, and electronic kanban boards allow agile practices even for distributed teams.

question: You're leading an agile team that has team members in various locations. You've arranged daily stand-ups via phone with electronic kanban and backlogs, but the team is not able to meet face-to-face. What else will you do to help the team increase team working together and getting to know each other?

options.A: Have a team board with their backgrounds and pictures
options.B: Swap roles throughout the team so each team member gets a feel for what others do
options.C: Pair up team members to work on tasks and rotate those pairs for each feature
options.D: Have a virtual lunch and learn where team members share something about themselves

explanation: Pair programming is a common technique in agile and can be used for most tasks; pairing up team members on a task or iteration gives them the opportunity to talk and work with each other one on one. Rotating pairs ensures different team members get to know each other across iterations.

question: You're showing up the project sponsor around the team area for your agile project and you see the burn down chart next to the team kanban board. The project sponsor asks what the chart tells you. What will you say?

options.A: It shows how many team members are moving on and off the project
options.B: It shows the remaining tasks versus what was planned for the iteration
options.C: It shows the automated tests run as part of continuous integration
options.D: It shows the return on investment for delivered features

explanation: A burn down chart is a line chart showing the iteration tasks completed versus what was planned over the iteration. The same idea can be used for features over the life of a project or even risks, and can be measured in hours, tasks, cards, or stories as the team agrees.

question: You're working through the feature backlog with the functional manager of the organization who is also the product owner for your agile team. They ask about the velocity chart hanging in the team area. What do you tell them?

options.A: The number of points, tasks or features completed by the team in each iteration
options.B: The number of team members working on the agile project
options.C: How fast each team member completes their work each day
options.D: The speed with which the project budget is being used up

explanation: Team velocity is the amount of work a team can complete in a given iteration, whether measured by points, hours, story cards, tasks, or features. The velocity chart shows the number of chosen units per iteration, making it essential for iteration planning.

question: In 2001, a group of individuals representing the most widely used lightweight software development methodologies agreed on a common set of values and principles which became known as the agile manifesto. Which of the following is one of the manifesto's core values?

options.A: Developer negotiation
options.B: Product owner interaction over scrum master delegation
options.C: Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
options.D: Getting things right over getting the right things

explanation: The agile manifesto is made up of four core values, one of which is customer collaboration over contract negotiation. This means valuing working closely with the customer and responding to their feedback rather than locking everything into a contract.

question: You're working with your project team in a way that ensures you analyze the requirements, then design, build, and test for those requirements, repeating them until the product is correct and adjusting your product or approach as necessary, before releasing in one large feature. Which project life cycle are you using?

options.A: Predictive
options.B: Agile
options.C: Incremental
options.D: Iterative

explanation: The iterative project lifecycle analyzes, designs, tests, builds, and repeats these activities until correct, then delivers in one release. Incremental delivers small increments of value (features), agile combines iterative and incremental, and predictive does one release with no feedback.

question: Large-scale scrum (LeSS) aims to organize several development teams toward a common goal by extending the scrum method across teams. What is NOT one of the similarities between large-scale scrum and normal scrum?

options.A: One single product backlog
options.B: One definition of done for all teams
options.C: Valuing individuals over results
options.D: One potentially shippable product increment at the end of each sprint

explanation: Large-scale scrum has many similarities to normal scrum extended across teams, including one single product backlog, one definition of done for all teams, complete cross-functional teams, one product owner, and one potentially shippable product increment at the end of each sprint. Valuing individuals over results is not a stated similarity, as both individuals and results are valued.

question: Large-scale scrum aims to organize several development teams towards a common goal by extending scrum across teams. Which of the techniques below was NOT added to scrum to create LeSS?

options.A: Sprint planning more formally divided into two parts: what and how
options.B: One single product release with no stakeholder feedback allowed
options.C: Overall cross-team refinement
options.D: Overall retrospective focused on cross-team improvements

explanation: Large-scale scrum adds techniques to scrum to scale across teams, including sprint planning divided into 'what and how', overall cross-team refinement, cross-team coordination, and cross-team retrospectives. A single release with no stakeholder feedback contradicts the agile principle of continuous feedback and was not added.

question: You're working on a project with clear procedures based on past projects. The product is clear and the risks are fairly well known as the organization has done this before. Which is true from the below?

options.A: It's definable work so you can choose a predictive project lifecycle
options.B: It's definable work so you can choose agile
options.C: High uncertainty means you can use incremental
options.D: High uncertainty means you can use iterative

explanation: When the situation is fairly definable and well known and the risks are also fairly well known, you can choose a predictive project lifecycle that plans most of the project up front with one single release. Agile is used to respond to change and high uncertainty, not low-risk definable work.

question: You are an agile project manager leading a project team who is used to a waterfall way of work. The project sponsor wants to plan the entire project up front so they can see when the project will be delivered. You tell them that agile uses adaptive planning. Why might this be a better alternative?

options.A: Adaptive planning allows the project sponsor to adapt to the project plan over time
options.B: Adaptive planning allows the team to deliver usable features while continually re-prioritizing the next pieces based on business value
options.C: Adaptive planning ensures that users can adapt to the product
options.D: Adaptive planning is stable after the prototype is complete

explanation: Adaptive planning allows the team to deliver usable features incrementally while the product owner continuously re-prioritizes the backlog based on business value, ensuring maximum value is delivered throughout the project. This aligns with agile's core principle of responding to change and delivering value iteratively.

question: You're a scrum master and you're about to begin a new iteration in your agile project. New features have been added to the backlog and the team are unsure which of those features to work on next. How will you guide the product owner and the team when analyzing the next priority?

options.A: Analyze the value of each feature with the product owner versus the effort and the risk
options.B: Focus on the shortest tasks first so you can deliver quickly
options.C: Choose to deliver the entire project at the end
options.D: By asking customers what they want delivered first

explanation: While value is typically determined by features that customers want, the priority is ultimately decided by the product owner who represents the customer or the business and constantly grooms and prioritizes the backlog of features. Just as important is developing high-risk items and considering the development effort versus the benefit.

question: You're doing great coaching some of the team members who are new to agile and someone asks what you mean when you say a time box. What will you tell them?

options.A: It is a period of time in which certain work is completed, such as an iteration
options.B: It is time spent on a retrospective
options.C: It is time taken to complete the project with the box representing the finished product
options.D: It is the effort assigned to a story card

explanation: While effort on a story card may be time boxed, the best example of a time box is an iteration, usually around two weeks, which is a time box that delivers a feature or part of a feature that a customer can see, feel, or touch. Time boxing is used to combat Parkinson's Law, where work expands to fill the time allotted.

question: While on an agile project team, you prioritize the backlog of features with the product owner and agree what you will work on first. The project sponsor wants to know how long it will take to deliver some of this value. How will you estimate how long each of these features will take to complete?

options.A: Break down the feature into tasks or user stories, then estimate how long those tasks will take to complete with the development team
options.B: Take the total number of people in the team multiplied by hours worked divided by the number of features
options.C: Ask the project sponsor how many iterations the project is funded for and then plan to that
options.D: Find a similar feature delivered by another part of the organization and use that as the estimate

explanation: Agile favors a bottom-up approach to estimating where work is broken down to its lowest level, typically a user story, and estimated by the development team including testers and business analysts. This gives the best estimate of the effort and time required to complete the feature.

question: You're working with an agile team on your first iteration. The feature has been chosen by the product owner and the team breaks it down into user stories to work on. What is a user story?

options.A: A feature or functionality small enough to be completed in one to three days
options.B: A description of the feature from the viewpoint of the customer
options.C: A story about the end user so the team understands the feature completely
options.D: A prioritized backlog

explanation: A user story is the smallest unit of work in an agile team — a task that can be completed in one to three days. If the task will take longer, it is common to try and break it into two or more pieces to accommodate that user story rule.

question: You've begun working on a new project in a new organization and the project team mentions you are working in an iterative project environment. How do you know you're working within an iterative project life cycle?

options.A: The team iterates around one idea to make sure that it's right
options.B: You are improving the product or result through successive prototypes or proofs of concept
options.C: The project sponsor says the project life cycle is iterative
options.D: You are using agile sprints or iterations to manage your work

explanation: An iterative project life cycle uses successive prototypes or proofs of concept to gather feedback and then releases in one go. Even though agile uses sprints or iterations, combining iterative and incremental approaches is what creates the agile way of work.

question: In 2001, a group of individuals representing the most widely used lightweight software development methodologies agreed on a common set of values and principles that became known as the Agile Manifesto. Which of the following is one of the Agile Manifesto's core values?

options.A: Developing as much as possible to see what gets the best result
options.B: Finding a product owner over finding a development team
options.C: Cross-functional team members over executive buy-in
options.D: Responding to change over following a plan

explanation: The Agile Manifesto is made up of four core values, one of which values responding to change over following a plan. This means following a plan is still important, however agile values responding to change a little bit more, because customers often change their minds once they get their hands on the product.

question: You are working on a project where the organization wants to move towards an agile way of work. Which two project life cycles combine to create an agile environment?

options.A: Iterative and predictive
options.B: Incremental and predictive
options.C: Iterative and incremental
options.D: Predictive and agile

explanation: An agile project environment is created by combining incremental (where smaller features are released more regularly) and iterative (where the product improves through iterations and is released after feedback). Predictive is the waterfall approach and does not combine to create an agile environment.

question: You have come on board a new project team and you've been advised that the project works with a predictive style of project life cycle. How will you know you're working in a predictive or waterfall style approach?

options.A: The bulk of the planning is done up front and then executed in a single sequential process
options.B: The bulk of planning is done up front, then prototypes are created
options.C: A product roadmap is created and smaller features are designed and released as part of the whole
options.D: The project uses feature sprints, kanban boards and retrospectives to manage their work

explanation: In a predictive or waterfall approach, all the planning is done up front and executed in a sequential process, ideal for definable work and products where the risk is well known. Prototypes indicate iterative, incremental releases indicate incremental, and agile tools like sprints and kanban are not specific to the project life cycle type.

question: You're working as a project manager in an agile team. Which of the below are NOT examples of execution practices you will see in agile?

options.A: Test at all levels where the team applies end-to-end testing and unit testing
options.B: Test driven development where we write the tests first and then write the code
options.C: Spikes which are time-boxed research or experiments useful for estimating or learning something critical
options.D: The project management plan where the project is planned in detail up front to avoid misunderstanding by project stakeholders

explanation: A project management plan is a key artifact in a predictive or waterfall project management approach, not an agile execution practice. Test at all levels, test driven development, and spikes are all agile execution practices.

question: The product owner has worked with the development team to list all the work to be done, write up the user stories for each feature and place them into the product backlog. What will you do next?

options.A: Groom the backlog to ensure only important items are being worked on
options.B: Create the schedule management plan
options.C: Sort the items by business priority, place them in an iteration, complete the items and then remove them from the backlog
options.D: Move the items into the requirements traceability matrix so you can report on their progress

explanation: Managing project requirements via the product backlog is a fundamental agile practice; the product backlog is the source of truth and is continuously reprioritized to ensure maximum value is delivered first. Items are sorted by business priority, placed in an iteration, completed, and then removed.

question: The business partner or product owner continuously refines or grooms the product backlog. This includes adding new stories, reprioritizing stories and removing stories when needed. What role does the development team take during this process?

options.A: Developing the cards as soon as they are added to ensure maximum value
options.B: Removing cards from the backlog that don't make sense to complete
options.C: Estimating the work needed for each card so the product owner can prioritize based on value and effort
options.D: Coming in to daily standups to report on what has been done yesterday and what will be done today

explanation: During backlog grooming, the development team is responsible for sizing or estimating the work; this allows the product owner to prioritize effectively based on value and time to delivery. The product owner needs to know the effort required to make sound prioritization decisions.

question: You are working with your agile project team to estimate each task and how long they might take. The team is finding it difficult to give an exact figure and worries that it might impact project delivery. What can you show them to estimate more accurately?

options.A: Ask them to estimate by whole iteration to give a little room for variation
options.B: Estimate the cards in full days instead of effort per card
options.C: Use comparative estimates or relative sizing such as t-shirt sizing or story points
options.D: Prioritize the backlog based on customer value

explanation: A common agile practice is to rely on relative sizing such as story points or t-shirt sizing; team velocity is then measured using those same methods as the team completes a certain number of points per iteration. This is easier than trying to predict an exact time period.

question: You're an agile project manager and talking through the iteration with the product owner. They have created the list of items in the product backlog but there is confusion in the team about who should perform the process of story card sizing. What will you tell them?

options.A: The product owner works through the backlog to size the story cards
options.B: The agile team work together to size the story cards relying mostly on information from the developers and testers
options.C: The agile team leader or scrum master works to size the story cards for the team
options.D: The customer sizes the story cards to ensure maximum value

explanation: The story point sizing process is performed by the agile team, primarily estimated by developers and testers who will be doing the work, so they will know best. Servant leadership means helping people do their best work, not doing everything for them.

question: You've been estimating story cards with your agile project team but the effort captured has not been matching the time it ends up taking to complete. What is the most likely reason for this occurring?

options.A: The team have not included sick days in the estimate
options.B: The team have not included testing or refactoring in the estimate
options.C: The team have not included the time to merge the code for continuous integration
options.D: The team have not included the time it takes for the daily stand up

explanation: Estimates for story cards should include all known activities required to complete that card, including testing and refactoring and the complexity and risk involved. Continuous integration merging should be automated, and daily standups are not part of card-level estimating.

question: You're an agile team leader and talking through the process for your first retrospective. The team is curious as to what they should do. What is the way a team retrospective would normally work?

options.A: Discuss what was successful, what could be improved, and incorporate those into future iterations
options.B: Discuss what the team liked about working with each other
options.C: Discuss what went wrong and make sure the team knows who is to blame
options.D: Discuss the validity of features being developed

explanation: A team retrospective enables the team to answer questions that improve their team process and way of work, such as what went well, what could be improved, and what still puzzles me. Blame is not a part of agile — retrospectives focus on improving the process, not the people.

question: The 12th principle of agile is: at regular intervals, the team reflects on how to become more effective, then tunes and adjusts its behavior accordingly. In practice this is a retrospective. What is the practical reason for including retrospectives in an agile team?

options.A: It reduces the communication time between the project sponsor and the developers
options.B: It ensures features are released on schedule and on budget
options.C: It helps the team learn about and improve their process over time
options.D: It keeps the team focused on the main feature to deliver the most business value

explanation: Agile retrospectives help the team learn about their process and improve it over time through regular and structured feedback given and used together as a team. Prioritization is handled by the product owner in sprint planning, not through retrospectives.

question: Retrospectives are typically held at the end of an iteration, but an agile team can hold retrospectives at any other time. What is NOT a time that an agile team might hold a retrospective?

options.A: When the team completes or ships something new
options.B: When more than a few weeks have passed since the previous retrospective
options.C: When the team appears to be stuck and completed work is not flowing
options.D: When the feature backlog has not been created and work is yet to begin

explanation: A team retrospective is there to gather feedback and improve on the team process once that process is in place; it is not held before the work or the process has begun as there is nothing to gather feedback on yet. Retrospectives require completed work to reflect upon.

question: You are in a new project team and working through which project life cycle or way of work to use in order to deliver the products to your customer and how far to modify that project lifecycle. What are some of the project factors that will influence your tailoring decisions to use agile or other methods?

options.A: Which project life cycle the customer wants to use
options.B: What the latest life cycle is, the one that must be the best and most interesting to upper management
options.C: Whether the flow of work is often interrupted by various delays or impediments and is easily definable or highly uncertain
options.D: Project approaches should never be tailored

explanation: Project life cycles and how much you tailor them depends on many factors; having definable work or high uncertainty work are two of the most common factors. Projects should always be tailored because organizations, products, people, markets, and industries differ.

question: You're in the forming stages of a new project team and deciding on the way of work. What are some of the factors that will influence how you tailor the product life cycle?

options.A: The demand pattern for value or delivery — is it steady or sporadic
options.B: The rate of process improvement required by the team — do we need retrospectives or can we deliver in one big bang
options.C: Is more than one team needed to build the product
options.D: All of the above

explanation: Project life cycles and ways of working can be influenced by many factors — in fact almost everything will impact this decision, including the demand pattern, rate of process improvement needed, team size, whether work is certain or uncertain, and what the team is comfortable with.

question: You're working through the backlog with the product owner and estimating the story cards with the development team. The team have never worked on a product like this before and they're unsure if the estimates are accurate enough. What will you recommend that they use to get accurate estimates?

options.A: Affinity estimating using actual effort from a similar feature on a different project
options.B: T-shirt sizing using broad small, medium or large sizes to accommodate variation
options.C: Three-point estimating where we have the pessimistic, most likely and optimistic and then average all of those out
options.D: The product owner should estimate the story cards

explanation: While most estimation methods will do, the best one in this case is affinity estimating using a similar example from a previous project. Agile estimating is most often done by breaking the features down into small tasks and then estimating on those tasks (bottom-up estimating); where that doesn't work, affinity estimating is the next best choice.

question: You're delivering features through your agile project and the project sponsor and product owner are happy with the progress. Your project sponsor raises a concern about future items and what that development will look like. What will you show them?

options.A: The project management plan
options.B: The product backlog
options.C: The product roadmap that shows planned future features and their approximate dates, similarly to a Gantt chart
options.D: The team velocity chart that shows how much the team completes each iteration

explanation: A product roadmap is a visual display of the product releases similar to a Gantt chart or a project schedule but based around agile feature releases; it is a high level view adjusted as necessary and usually done by the product owner.

question: The agile team is estimating story cards using planning poker for one of the larger features. Some of the team have estimated three points while the others have estimated eight points. How will you guide them to come to an agreement?

options.A: Ask them to average out the points and assign the value to the card
options.B: Change the method of estimating to accommodate the team decision
options.C: Change the feature to make it easier to estimate on
options.D: Ask the high and the low estimators to discuss their reasons and the team to estimate again until a consensus is reached or more information is needed

explanation: In planning poker, if the estimates are different, the high and low estimators share their reasons; once shared, the team estimates again, and that is repeated until a consensus is reached or the team decides to gather more information.

question: The agile team is working through a particularly complex feature that is difficult for the team to find a solution for. As the agile lead you recommend a spike. What is a spike?

options.A: A shifting of the feature into another iteration
options.B: A time-boxed task to explore or investigate an issue
options.C: A short meeting at the beginning of the day to discuss tasks in progress
options.D: A demonstration of the feature to the product owner

explanation: A spike is a time-boxed task to explore or investigate an issue; it is separate from development work and is only used if something needs to be figured out or investigated.

question: You've been completing small usable increments of value as part of your agile team. When reporting back to the project sponsor, the last iteration was referred to as a 'fast failure'. What does that actually mean?

options.A: The iteration did not complete all the story cards in the allotted time
options.B: The project team found a bug before code delivery
options.C: The proof of concept for that iteration did not have the intended outcome
options.D: The product backlog was not well groomed to start the next iteration

explanation: Agile operates on the concept of delivering increments of value; 'fast failure' means that particular increment was not successful and did not deliver what was wanted, so the team needs to adjust. It is part of the beauty and power of agile.

question: Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) focuses on roles and activities in the portfolio, program and project levels and organizes the enterprise around value streams that provide value to the customer. What is NOT one of the principles of SAFe?

options.A: Take an economic view and apply systems thinking
options.B: Visualize and limit the work in progress and reduce batch sizes
options.C: Plan carefully for a large single feature release
options.D: Build incrementally with fast integrated learning cycles

explanation: SAFe uses agile principles but scales them across teams and across the organization; these include systems thinking, visualizing work in progress, and building incrementally. Planning long-term for a single release is a predictive way of work, not an agile or SAFe principle.

question: Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) again focuses on detailing practices, roles and activities in the portfolio, program and project levels and organizes the enterprise around value streams. What is NOT one of the SAFe principles?

options.A: Make sure that stakeholders know when you did more work than your teammates
options.B: Apply the cadence for synchronizing with cross-domain planning
options.C: Unlock the intrinsic motivation of knowledge workers
options.D: Decentralize decision making

explanation: Agile focuses on individuals and interactions over processes and tools; making sure stakeholders know when you did more work than teammates is not a SAFe or agile principle. SAFe principles include cadence and synchronization, intrinsic motivation, and decentralized decision making.

question: Your agile project team has been delivering value to an organization in increments for some time. Executives within the organization would like to scale the approach across more teams to gain benefit from its way of work. Which of the below is NOT a scaling framework from agile that you might use?

options.A: Scrum of scrums
options.B: SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework)
options.C: Kanban iteration retrospective
options.D: Large Scale Scrum (LeSS)

explanation: Scrum of scrums, Scaled Agile Framework, and Large Scale Scrum are all scaling agile frameworks. 'Kanban iteration retrospective' is not a real scaling methodology.

question: The organization you're working in has decided to move towards an agile way of work and project life cycle approach. Which of the below is how you would describe an agile project life cycle?

options.A: Both iterative and incremental to refine work items and deliver frequently
options.B: Both iterative and predictive
options.C: Both fast and furious designed to deliver the most bang for your buck
options.D: Both predictive and incremental

explanation: The agile project life cycle is a combination of iterative (where we gather feedback) and incremental (where we deliver smaller usable features of value to our customer). Predictive approaches plan everything up front and deliver in one go, which is not agile.

question: There are four main project life cycle approaches to managing a project with variations in between. What is one of the characteristics of an iterative life cycle approach?

options.A: It does not allow feedback
options.B: It allows feedback for unfinished work to improve and modify that work
options.C: It focuses on delivery not feedback
options.D: It plans the project up front and delivers in one go

explanation: An iterative project life cycle iterates towards success; it uses prototypes and unreleased versions of the product to gather feedback and improve it before releasing in one go. PMBoC Guide citation: iterative life cycle.

question: You're working through a sprint planning meeting with your team. The team has sized up the cards and prioritized the backlog. What will you do next to determine how much work can be done during this iteration?

options.A: Count up the user stories that the product owner wants completed by the end of the iteration
options.B: Check the team's velocity against the number of points for the story cards that we want to complete
options.C: Ask the scrum master to allocate work to each team member in the fairest manner possible
options.D: Look at the other teams and what they did in the last iteration to give us an idea for our team

explanation: Velocity is the average amount of work a team completes over previous sprints, measured in story points or another agreed unit, and comparing it against the planned story card points tells you how much can be completed in the upcoming sprint. Other teams' velocity is not relevant as every team measures differently.

question: You're an agile project manager working on the next feature for your product. The product owner has asked you for an estimate on how long the remainder of the features may take to complete. You look to the team's velocity chart. Why is this?

options.A: The team's average velocity across previous iterations is the best predictor of future iterations
options.B: The velocity ensures the team will never work harder than they have to
options.C: The scrum master has the final say over team velocity to ensure the work gets done
options.D: The velocity chart shows how quickly new items are picked up by the team

explanation: The average velocity or work completed during each iteration is the best predictor of future velocity because previous iterations typically include all those tasks and pitfalls that might be encountered again. Velocity is a measurement of work flow, not a cap set by the scrum master.

question: You are the agile lead or scrum master for your agile team and planning the next iteration that is about to begin. What will you do before planning the next iteration?

options.A: Break down the features into user stories
options.B: Write the story card acceptance criteria within the team
options.C: Choose which stories the team will work on in the iteration
options.D: Work with the product owner to prioritize the highest value ones to work on

explanation: During iteration planning, the team should have already broken down features into story cards; now is the time to re-prioritize those cards based on business value and team velocity. Breaking down features and writing acceptance criteria are done by the development team, business analysts, and product owner.

question: You are in the middle of a sprint with your agile team and moving the project forward with daily stand-up meetings. What is a stand-up meeting?

options.A: A project kickoff meeting
options.B: A project progress meeting to see how the proposed schedule stands up against the actual schedule
options.C: A very quick meeting time-boxed at 15 minutes or less where the team says what they have accomplished and report any blockers
options.D: A team training meeting

explanation: The daily stand-up is a time-boxed 15-minute meeting held once a day where team members report what they have done, what they plan to do, and most importantly any blockers preventing progress. It is held around the kanban board and focuses on identifying and resolving impediments quickly.

question: You are the agile project manager working on a software project and the development team has identified a major risk for the delivery of a certain feature. That feature is not scheduled until very late in the project. What will you do next?

options.A: Wait until later in the project as the problem may be fixed by other features going in
options.B: Ask the developers to find a way to proceed without that feature
options.C: Plan a spike around the risk to identify a solution, then prioritize that feature earlier in development
options.D: Remove all other story cards from the current sprint so the team can swarm around the issue and fix it

explanation: Agile risks are prioritized early in development to reduce and remove them; discovery work is typically done with a spike card, and the solution is then prioritized earlier so it is addressed without stopping delivery of value. The longer a risk remains unaddressed the more expensive it becomes to fix.

question: Your agile project team is using scrum to manage the work and deliverables for your project. Which of the below is NOT one of the scrum events or artifacts?

options.A: The product backlog
options.B: The sprint backlog
options.C: Increments or features
options.D: The team area

explanation: Scrum works with a product backlog and sprint backlog as artifacts and delivers in increments or features; agile teams are typically recommended to work in a co-located space, but the team area is not one of the defined events or artifacts from scrum itself.

question: Your agile team has been working with scrum for some time now and have become quite familiar with its events and ceremonies. You're asked to explain this to other stakeholders. What is NOT an example of a scrum event or ceremony?

options.A: Sprint planning
options.B: The daily scrum or stand-up
options.C: The sprint review
options.D: The change control board

explanation: Sprint planning, the daily scrum, and the sprint review are all scrum ceremonies; the change control board is a typical part of a predictive or waterfall project life cycle, not an agile or scrum way of work.

question: Scrum is a single team framework for managing product development. There are certain events, ceremonies, and roles and responsibilities within a scrum team. Which of the below is NOT a role or responsibility in a scrum team?

options.A: Product owner
options.B: Development team
options.C: Scrum master
options.D: Risk manager

explanation: Risk and quality are the responsibility of the whole team in an agile way of work; the three roles in scrum are the product owner, the development team, and the scrum master — a dedicated risk manager is a role associated with predictive project life cycles.

question: Agile servant leadership is one of the key foundations to an agile team where leadership, team participation, and openness are encouraged. Which of the below is NOT one of the characteristics of a servant leader?

options.A: Promoting self-awareness and helping people grow
options.B: Listening to others more than speaking yourself
options.C: Serving those on the team by removing blockers
options.D: Reporting on low performers to ensure the team is streamlined

explanation: Agile and servant leadership work by building towards psychological safety, openness, growth, and ensuring work is free from blockers; reporting on low performers is not a servant leadership characteristic — the goal is to grow and coach team members, not assess and report them.

question: Your team are about to proceed with the development of a new product for the organization. Depending on the product, stakeholders, risk appetite, need for value, and more, the life cycle or way of work will change. Which of the below represents an incremental life cycle approach?

options.A: It provides unfinished deliverables
options.B: It provides value in one release
options.C: It provides multiple finished deliverables that the customer can use immediately
options.D: It provides a way for the team to do less with more by releasing only a small feature

explanation: An incremental project life cycle or way of work delivers small increments that a customer can use immediately, gain value from, and provide feedback on. Deliverables must be complete and usable, and value is delivered through multiple releases rather than a single big-bang delivery.

question: You are working with your agile team to size story cards and place them in upcoming iterations. You notice that the points allocated to the story cards are getting bigger and bigger with each release. Why might this be happening?

options.A: The product owner keeps adding scope to the project
options.B: Technical debt is being built on from previous releases
options.C: Team members are not working their full hours
options.D: Team members are working too hard and being burnt out

explanation: Agile accommodates changing scope through prioritization, ruling out A and D; the best explanation is technical debt, where code is rushed to production without being simplified or refactored, creating more and more work to build around the disorganized code with each subsequent release.

question: The project sponsor of your agile team is quite familiar with lean and agile terms and methods. She has asked you for the average cycle time and the average lead time for the project so far. What will you give her?

options.A: The average time for team leave and team attendance
options.B: Time to complete stand-ups and time to complete retrospectives
options.C: The average time to complete story cards and the time to complete features
options.D: The average team downtime and the average time to complete releases

explanation: Cycle time is the time for shorter tasks — most often story cards in an agile environment — while lead time measures the time to complete a feature (a collection of story cards) from beginning to end; these are key lean metrics (PMBOK/Agile Practice Guide).

question: You are going over the velocity of your agile team and notice it has increased over the past two iterations. What might be the reason for this?

options.A: The productivity of one team member has increased
options.B: The team no longer tests each card
options.C: The team estimated the story cards more accurately
options.D: The product owner removed some features from the backlog of work

explanation: Velocity and throughput measure the work completed by the entire team; if one team member's productivity increases it lifts the whole team's velocity. Removing backlog items or re-estimating cards does not increase completed points per sprint.

question: You are an agile project manager working with your agile team and talking through the defects raised on the project so far. To get a more accurate view of effort per card you ask the quality tester to begin tracking the defect cycle time. What will they report back on?

options.A: How many defects were discovered
options.B: How long it took to fix a defect from the time it was found to the time it was fixed
options.C: How many cycles it takes to raise a defect
options.D: The number of features in the backlog that have experienced defects

explanation: Cycle time is the measure of time for a smaller increment — often a story card — from start to finish; applied to defects, it is the elapsed time from when a defect is found to when it is fixed (Agile Practice Guide / Lean metrics).

question: You are checking in with the quality tester in your agile team and they advise you that there are a number of escaped defects in the most recently completed feature. What are they referring to?

options.A: A feature that was not completed by the end of the iteration
options.B: Code that needs to be refactored
options.C: Defects that have made it through testing and quality assurance
options.D: Team members who did not attend the daily stand-up

explanation: Escaped defects are defects that pass through testing and release into the live (production) environment; the cost and risk increases the closer they are to customers, making them the most expensive type of defect to fix (Agile Practice Guide).

question: Agile approaches emphasize servant leadership to empower teams. Servant leadership is the practice of leading through service to the team, understanding and addressing the needs and development of team members to enable the highest performance possible. Servant leaders approach project work in this order:

options.A: Purpose, people and process
options.B: People's systems and technology
options.C: Information, reporting and process
options.D: Plant, people and machine

explanation: A servant leader starts with the 'why' — the purpose behind the work — then grows and develops people, and finally ensures a solid process for making the work happen; this mirrors Simon Sinek's 'Start with Why' and the PMI Agile Practice Guide servant leadership model.

question: You have been part of an agile team for some time and working on the aspects of servant leadership to improve the results in your team. What is NOT one of the characteristics of a servant leader?

options.A: Coaching versus controlling
options.B: Promoting safety
options.C: Promoting the energy and intelligence of others
options.D: Ensuring the team does what you say

explanation: The servant leader in agile coaches the team, promotes psychological safety and trust, and promotes the intelligence and energy of others; directive leadership or forcing others to do what you say is not part of a servant leadership approach (PMI Agile Practice Guide).

question: You have delivered half of the features in your backlog for your agile project. You've noticed there's quite a difference in the number of defects over the last three iterations. You ask the team to investigate both the common cause and the special cause variation. What will they investigate?

options.A: Issues that arose from normal operations and issues that arose from a unique or new condition
options.B: Issues caused by the common area and issues caused by the special software
options.C: Issues found in the last release and issues found in this release
options.D: Issues caused by continuous integration and issues caused by no integration

explanation: In lean and the Toyota Production System, variation is separated into common cause (normal, everyday operations) and special cause (special or new factors outside normal operations). Citation: Lean/Toyota Production System variation terminology.

question: You have built an experienced agile team and have delivered constantly for your organization. The product owner who has been with you since the beginning has taken another job and a new teammate with very little experience joins the team. Which of the below will you NOT tell them regarding the product owner role?

options.A: The product owner represents the customer and generates, maintains, and prioritizes the backlog to ensure the highest business value without creating waste
options.B: The product owner must have previous experience in agile to work in the team
options.C: The product owner may work with stakeholders, customers, and the teams to define the product direction and rank the work based on business value
options.D: Product owners typically have a business background and bring deep subject matter expertise to their decisions

explanation: The product owner role requires deep expertise in the business or customer domain, but does not require previous agile experience — that can be coached and taught. Citation: Agile product owner role definition.

question: The intent of the agile unified process is to perform more iterative cycles across seven key disciplines and incorporate the associated feedback before formal delivery. Which of the following is NOT a principle that guides AUP disciplines?

options.A: Tailoring the approach to fit
options.B: Ensuring the team knows what it is doing
options.C: Collective agreement of scope before delivery
options.D: Focus on high value activities

explanation: Agile Unified Process principles mirror agile values: tailor your approach, ensure the team knows what it is doing (using generalizing specialists), and focus on highest-value activities first. Collective agreement of scope before delivery is not an AUP principle — scope is managed by the product owner, not by collective pre-delivery agreement. Citation: Agile Unified Process (AUP) principles.

question: The 12 clarifying principles in agile were defined to give more clarity to the agile manifesto and core values. Which of the following is one of the 12 clarifying principles?

options.A: Our highest priority is to satisfy the customer through early and continuous delivery of valuable software
options.B: The highest priority is to ensure executive buy-in by delivering high-value items
options.C: Our highest priority is to reduce the impact of change by keeping information to a minimum
options.D: Our high priority is to plan thoroughly and execute with discipline

explanation: One of the 12 agile clarifying principles is that the highest priority is to satisfy the customer through early and continuous delivery of valuable software (or knowledge work). The other options reflect waterfall or non-agile thinking. Citation: Agile Manifesto 12 Clarifying Principles.

question: You are working with a new agile team and have reviewed the team velocity over the past three iterations. The maximum number of points completed was 35 and the fewest was 25. What will you do next?

options.A: Push the team to complete 5 more than the maximum and roll over any incomplete cards to the next iteration
options.B: Set 35 as the work in progress (WIP) limit and adjust it during future iterations if the team is able to pull more work
options.C: Add more people to the team to complete the work because 35 is not enough
options.D: Reduce the number of features in the backlog to suit the team velocity

explanation: Agile works on continuous, sustainable delivery; pushing beyond the team's known limits is not agile practice. Setting the WIP limit at the maximum achieved velocity (35) ensures the team is not overburdened, and can be adjusted upward if the team completes all work. Citation: Agile sustainable pace and WIP limit principles.

question: You're the product owner in an agile team and have a list of features in a backlog that need to be developed. Each feature is assigned a dollar value representing the return on investment. You would like to create a risk-adjusted backlog. What will you do next?

options.A: Note the risk impacts in dollars against the return in dollars, then re-prioritize the backlog
options.B: Adjust the backlog to focus on the largest features first
options.C: Capture known risks for each feature and then place cards to control those risks and prioritize them in the backlog
options.D: Create a risk register for the project and have it signed off by the risk owners

explanation: A risk-adjusted backlog is created by raising risk response tasks as cards and prioritizing them against normal feature cards, so risks are addressed early in the project. Creating a risk register and sign-off is a waterfall approach. Citation: Agile risk-adjusted backlog practices.

question: You're an agile project manager analyzing the risk for each feature in your backlog by assigning a probability and an impact score to each risk, then multiplying them to calculate risk severity. How will the team handle items with the highest risk?

options.A: Place all the high risk items in a dedicated risk iteration
options.B: Schedule them earlier in the project to reduce that risk impact over time
options.C: Schedule them later in the project
options.D: Change the requirements to reduce or remove the risk

explanation: Risk is highest early in the project before items have been completed; by completing high-risk items first you significantly reduce those risks and the threat of unknowns later in the project. Citation: Agile risk management — risk-adjusted backlog prioritization.

question: The scrum master tells you that agile builds problem solving into its process in a number of ways using transparency tools and ceremonies. Which of the following is NOT something that is designed to reveal and solve problems?

options.A: Retrospectives
options.B: Writing user stories
options.C: Iteration demonstrations and reviews
options.D: Raising blockers in the daily stand-up

explanation: Retrospectives, sprint reviews, and daily stand-ups are all specifically designed to reveal and solve problems during product delivery. Writing user stories is a useful practice but is not itself designed for revealing and solving problems. Citation: Agile ceremonies and transparency tools.

question: The whole team approach and cross-functional teams are core to agile. The team facilitator is a key member within the whole team. What is NOT correct regarding the team facilitator role?

options.A: The team facilitator can be called project manager, scrum master, project lead, team coach, or portfolio manager
options.B: The team facilitator must have a degree in business
options.C: All agile teams need servant leadership on the team
options.D: Team members should build up their own servant leadership skills of facilitation, coaching, and removing blockers

explanation: The team facilitator role is to facilitate discussion and elicit collaboration within the team; no specific degree is required — the role can be performed by anyone on the team with the right servant leadership skills. Citation: Agile whole team approach — team facilitator role.

question: You're rearranging a current agile team and you know that agile teams are cross-functional. What sort of team members will you select as you rearrange the team?

options.A: Generalizing specialists with one focus specialty — a T-shaped person with one deep specialty and a wide range of experience in other skills
options.B: Team leaders with a specialty in leadership so everyone can lead
options.C: Risk specialists
options.D: Do not add team members — shrink the team

explanation: An agile team is made up of generalizing specialists or T-shaped people who have a broad range of knowledge across disciplines and one deep specialty, enabling cross-functional delivery. Citation: Agile cross-functional team composition — T-shaped / generalizing specialists.

question: You are working through the iteration with your agile team and are having some trouble completing one of the story cards in the sprint. It is dependent on something else being done and the contact is away. What will you do next?

options.A: Raise a blocker at the next daily stand-up so the team can swarm around the problem and solve it quickly.
options.B: Bring the piece of work in-house so you don't have to worry anymore.
options.C: Ask the product owner to remove that feature for now and revisit it later.
options.D: Add cost to the budget and find a third party to complete the work.

explanation: In agile, blockers are raised as soon as possible — at least once a day during the daily stand-up — so the team can swarm around the problem, get help from any experts needed, and move on. This leverages the whole-team approach to problem-solving.

question: The product owner representing the business on your agile project is used to a more dictatorship style of leadership, telling people what to do. You explain that servant leadership is used in agile to ensure a safe and open environment. Why is this important to product development?

options.A: So the agile team lead knows who to blame when things go wrong.
options.B: So the team can accurately count the defects.
options.C: So the team feels free to ask for help, admit to problems or failures, and to improve.
options.D: So the entire team can push work on each other if they know they have time.

explanation: Psychological safety is one of the most important parts of a high-functioning team, meaning having the safety to be vulnerable, admit to problems, ask for help, and improve. It starts with the leader, and servant leadership contributes directly to this safe environment.

question: Feedback in many different forms is one of the most important parts of a successful agile product delivery. Which of the below is an agile method for gathering and using feedback to improve?

options.A: Having a once-weekly working group meeting.
options.B: Conducting sprint reviews or demonstrations that include all stakeholders.
options.C: Sending out project reports to project stakeholders to view.
options.D: Asking stakeholders for feedback only when development is blocked.

explanation: Demonstrating the usable increment developed during an iteration to the customer or product owner ensures feedback is gathered more quickly than only at the end of a project. Retrospectives are another agile way to gather and act on feedback.

question: You are working on an agile project where the product business representative is pushing for more and more features to be delivered in each iteration. You know what they are asking is not possible based on your team's current velocity. What will you do next?

options.A: Show them the team velocity and the points allotted to the cards in the backlog, then ask them to prioritize the features at the next sprint planning.
options.B: Add more members to the team.
options.C: Ask for another business representative who understands the technology better.
options.D: Remove some of the features yourself and see if anyone notices.

explanation: An agile team ultimately serves the customer. Showing the business representative the team velocity versus the points allotted gives them control over what is actually delivered within the team's capacity, making everyone feel better about what is happening.

question: You are working on a traditional waterfall project and the functional manager wants to get the benefit of certain agile tools to uncover potential problems before they occur. What will you do next?

options.A: Write the change into your project management plan and circulate it with project stakeholders.
options.B: Set a weekly working group meeting to discuss the project.
options.C: Start iteration planning.
options.D: Make the product backlog, iteration kanban board, and velocity chart all visible in a co-located team area.

explanation: Visual management is the best agile tool to detect and prevent problems. A visible kanban board, prioritized backlog, and velocity chart can be integrated seamlessly into a waterfall environment as a hybrid approach, allowing everyone to see what the team is up to and spot blockers early.

question: The agile manifesto has 12 clarifying principles added to it to help projects and teams understand the agile values. Which of the below is a correct clarifying principle?

options.A: Tailor the project approach to suit the project and the team.
options.B: Ensure executive buy-in.
options.C: Deliver working software frequently, from a couple of weeks to a couple of months, with a preference to the shorter time scale.
options.D: Ensure complete planning.

explanation: One of the 12 agile clarifying principles is to deliver working software frequently, from a couple of weeks to a couple of months, with a preference to the shorter time scale. This is a direct clarifying principle that adds detail to the agile manifesto values.

question: The agile manifesto has 12 clarifying principles. What is a correct 12th clarifying principle?

options.A: Business people should dictate to the development team what to do.
options.B: Developers can code and release products at a schedule that suits them for complete autonomy.
options.C: Business people and developers mustn't work together.
options.D: Business people and developers must work together daily throughout the project.

explanation: One of the agile clarifying principles states that business people and developers must work together daily throughout the project. From the various agile approaches, the product owner who represents the business must collaborate with developers daily, and when this happens, engagement and speed increase significantly.

question: The agile manifesto has 12 clarifying principles added to it to help projects and teams understand the agile values. Which of the below is a correct clarifying principle?

options.A: Build projects around a core idea, then brainstorm frequently to get the job done.
options.B: Find the right executive to support and ensure they tell you the product requirements.
options.C: Deliver frequently whether an item has been tested or not, as quality is everyone's responsibility.
options.D: Build projects around motivated individuals and give them the environment and support they need, and trust them to get the job done.

explanation: Agile is about building projects around motivated individuals — cross-functional or T-shaped people with a broad range of expertise and one deep specialty — giving them the support they need by removing blockers and ensuring a flow of work. This is a direct agile clarifying principle.

question: The 12 agile clarifying principles also match up with the best advice for projects, leadership, and communication. Which of the below is a correct clarifying principle when it comes to project communication?

options.A: The most efficient and effective method of conveying information to and within a development team is face-to-face communication.
options.B: Communication is always done in writing to ensure proof.
options.C: Agile teams ensure downward communication only, so the executive in charge directs the product features and the work.
options.D: When communicating, ensure that various forms such as telephone, writing, and face-to-face are used to ensure variety.

explanation: An agile team knows that communicating close in person, place, and time is the most efficient and effective way — often in a co-located team so other team members can pick up information by osmosis. Face-to-face communication is faster, picks up non-verbal cues, and reduces misinterpretation.

question: The agile manifesto has 12 clarifying principles. Here is another clarifying principle. Which of the below is a correct agile clarifying principle?

options.A: Union attendance is required to ensure team welfare.
options.B: Working software is the primary measure of progress.
options.C: Finding risks is the only way to avoid failure.
options.D: Learning new things helps keep a team engaged.

explanation: In an agile team, working software or a working product — increments and features delivered — is the primary measure of progress. Delivering in increments and features and getting customer feedback every time is central to the agile approach.

question: You're new to your agile project and the team are working through the forming stages. How do you help the team improve as the project progresses?

options.A: Ensure the team holds a project post-mortem and catches the lessons learned by the end of the project
options.B: Hold a retrospective with the whole team at the end of each iteration and build those lessons learned into each new iteration
options.C: Ask the product owner to show the team where they can improve
options.D: Rotate tasks and roles during each iteration

explanation: Agile retrospectives held at the end of each iteration ask the whole team what went well, what did not go well, what did they learn, and what still puzzles them; the lessons are actioned and built back into future iterations to continuously improve. This is more effective than end-of-project post-mortems and keeps the whole team involved.

question: You are the agile project manager working through the product requirements for the product owner and executive project sponsor. The project sponsor raises concerns that the development team do not fully understand the requirements. What will you do next?

options.A: Show them the project management plan which will have the scope statement listed in the scope baseline
options.B: Ask the team to create a high level prototype of the requirements and then discuss and review that with all stakeholders
options.C: Show the team the project schedule outlining the delivery dates
options.D: Hold a retrospective to gather feedback and improve

explanation: Agile uses visual mock-ups or prototypes such as storyboards, wireframes, or models to get a quick understanding of requirements and consensus from everyone involved. Creating a high level prototype allows the team to see, discuss, and collectively agree on requirements.

question: The scrum master on your agile project has asked you for the total lead time of a particular feature. You add up the cycle time of all the cards that contributed to that feature. What other two factors must you take into consideration when calculating total lead time?

options.A: Time for testing the code and time for merging the code
options.B: Meeting times and lunch times
options.C: Value-added time and non-value-added time
options.D: Sprint planning time and review time

explanation: Lead time is a concept from lean and the Toyota Production System; when calculating cycle time and lead time you must take into consideration both value-added time (directly working on stories contributing to the customer outcome) and non-value-added time (waste such as waiting, handoffs, and context switching). Both must be accounted for to get an accurate total lead time.

question: When working in an agile team it is common to use prototypes, demonstrations and reviews, wireframes, and storyboards to elicit feedback from the customer or product owner. What is the benefit of using these tools?

options.A: The development team don't have to create the real thing
options.B: The team can get together and have another meeting
options.C: It allows the users to create new forms that will be required for the new system
options.D: It allows the product owner to discuss and agree on requirements with their stakeholders

explanation: Prototypes, wireframes, and storyboards are important ways to gather feedback that allows all stakeholders to discuss and agree on the requirements and product features before the money is spent on actual development. This ensures shared understanding and reduces rework.

question: You're finishing up the first iteration of your agile project and have set a retrospective for the whole team. One member of the team asks why they have this meeting and if they really need to go. What will you tell them?

options.A: It helps the team prioritize product features for the next iteration
options.B: It allows the team to blame others when things go wrong
options.C: It's an opportunity for the whole team to look at their way of work and improve
options.D: It showcases a part of the product so everyone can see what was delivered

explanation: Retrospectives are a set meeting at the end of each sprint asking for feedback with questions such as what went well, what didn't go well, what did I learn, and what still puzzles me; the actions from these are built back into the process to continuously improve. The whole team must be involved as everyone may have different feedback.

question: You're in the beginning stages of a new project where the organization would like to use agile to manage the project work. The executive manager asks you how the team will make quality everyone's responsibility, leverage the various skill sets within the team, and remove reliance on external inputs. What agile method is she describing?

options.A: Servant leadership
options.B: Sprint planning
options.C: The daily stand up
options.D: The whole team approach

explanation: The whole team approach aims to bring all necessary people into the project team, ideally in a co-located space; it involves cross-functional team members who specialise in one skill and are fluent enough in a wide range of other skills, reducing silos and handoffs. This makes quality everyone's responsibility and removes reliance on external inputs.

question: You're working in an agile project for some time and have been blocked by the need for expertise in another team. What do you do to overcome organizational silos?

options.A: Work with the various managers of team members you need to have them dedicate the necessary individuals to our cross-functional team
options.B: Raise a change request for review by the project change control board as the work will be delayed
options.C: Raise an issue in the issue log for review and management by the project team
options.D: Work with the risk team to raise a project risk and assign an owner to it and create controls for those risks

explanation: This describes the whole team approach where all the necessary team members and their skills are brought into the project team, reducing silos and handoffs and making sure the project can be delivered as one team. Raising change requests, issue logs, or risk registers are old-school waterfall approaches, not agile.

question: The 12 agile clarifying principles also match up with the best advice for workflow and getting things done. Which of the below is a correct clarifying principle when it comes to project workflow?

options.A: Agile does not use processes as it prefers individuals and interactions over processes and tools
options.B: Agile processes engage and support the team by ensuring sponsors, developers, and users all get what they want
options.C: Agile processes promote sustainable development; the sponsors, developers, and users should be able to maintain a constant pace indefinitely
options.D: Agile processes help as much work get done as possible; when the team thinks they can't go further agile pushes them to go the extra mile

explanation: Agile promotes sustainable development — be wary of often switching tasks or pushing for too much work during an iteration as this will burn out individuals and ultimately slow down progress. Sponsors, developers, and users should be able to maintain a constant pace indefinitely.

question: The 12 agile clarifying principles also match up with the best quality advice for quality. Which of the below is a correct agile clarifying principle when it comes to project quality?

options.A: Continuous flow ensures work is done at a reasonable pace and defects are caught
options.B: Testing is done at all levels from unit testing to end to end testing
options.C: Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design enhances quality
options.D: Quality is everyone's responsibility in an agile team meaning everyone will test the product

explanation: The agile clarifying principle states that continuous attention to technical excellence — for example extreme programming principles — and good design enhances agility and quality. While quality is everyone's responsibility and testing is important, only continuous attention to technical excellence and good design is the actual clarifying principle from this list.

question: The 12 agile clarifying principles also match up with the best advice for keeping things simple in a project. Which of the below is a correct clarifying principle?

options.A: Simplicity is only possible when the team first understands how complex the system is
options.B: Simplicity is part of every project; everyone naturally simplifies the work as part of their role
options.C: Simplicity reduces cost in a project ensuring executives are always simplifying the process and the system
options.D: Simplicity — the art of maximizing the amount of work not done — is essential

explanation: The agile clarifying principle on simplicity states that simplicity — the art of maximizing the amount of work not done — is essential; this is Occam's razor applied to project work, making things as simple as possible but no simpler. The other options do not match the actual clarifying principle.

question: You are going through a retrospective with your team at the end of the sprint where the team have raised an issue that is slowing down their work. The scrum master guides the team to go through an exercise called the five whys. What is the purpose of this?

options.A: To ask who, what, where and when to find the cause of the problem
