options.B: To ask why the issue happened and then why for each reason after that until the cause is found
options.C: To ask why the team is working on each feature to give the team purpose
options.D: To allocate five whys to each story card to ensure the team knows the reason for working on it

explanation: The five whys is a problem solving technique from lean or the Toyota Production System that asks why a problem occurred; for each answer the team asks why again until they get to the real cause of the issue. It is used during retrospectives to find the root cause of problems slowing the team down.

question: The scrum master in an agile team has asked the team to do a self-assessment. What is the focus of this self-assessment?

options.A: How often the team meets during the week
options.B: How many story cards the team have completed
options.C: How much yearly bonus the team should receive
options.D: How the team performs and delivers together

explanation: The team self-assessment is used to gauge the team members' performance together, with results helping to identify what is working well and where improvements can be made; the team then meets to problem solve improvements and celebrate what is working well. It is a key agile practice for continuous improvement.

question: You're working in an agile team and the business representative would like to get an idea of the risk associated with a particular feature. What will you do next?

options.A: Show the risk management plan from your project management plan to the business representative
options.B: Ask the development team if they like the feature
options.C: Hold a premortem where each team member writes reasons they think the task or feature might fail so these can be discussed
options.D: Check your gut feel for the feature and if it doesn't feel right look into it more deeply

explanation: A premortem looks at reasons for failure before they happen and is a great way to brainstorm risk for a card or feature; typically team members write their reasons down and discuss them round-robin style, then group similar ideas together using an affinity diagram. This is the agile approach to risk identification for a specific feature.

question: You're the agile team leader and are working through a team retrospective when an issue experienced by the team is raised. What will you do next?

options.A: Perform fishbone analysis
options.B: Kano analysis
options.C: Regression analysis
options.D: Multi-vary chart analysis

explanation: When an issue is raised, the best course of action is to get to the root cause; of the options presented, fishbone (Ishikawa diagram) analysis is the best tool for root cause analysis. It uses brainstorming buckets such as people, information, process and system (PIPS) to identify the underlying causes of a problem.

question: You've been hired to lead a new project team as an agile project manager. You come on board to learn that the organization uses a hybrid model. What does this mean?

options.A: The team is a hybrid of developers, designers and business users to get the best return on investment
options.B: The product you are developing will use both electricity and fuel for power like a hybrid car
options.C: A combination of predictive (for example a project plan planned in advance) and adaptive (for example iterations, backlogs, kanban) project methods will be used
options.D: A hybrid team environment of physical and software reporting boards will be used

explanation: A hybrid project management method means using predictive and adaptive methods together; predictive brings traditional waterfall elements such as pre-planned project plans, scope, schedule and risk, while adaptive adds a prioritized backlog of features, kanban boards, iteration planning and retrospectives. This combined approach is common in organizations transitioning to agile.

question: You're the product owner in an agile team working with the developers to create story cards to place in the product backlog. With the three C's of user story creation, what do you do first?

options.A: Create the card, which is the physical or software driven media describing a user story
options.B: Confirm the acceptance criteria
options.C: Have a conversation with the team to flesh out those requirements
options.D: Catch defects before they're developed by working with quality testers before and after

explanation: The three C's of user story creation are Card, Conversation and Confirmation — in that order; you first create the card (the physical or digital media describing the user story), then have a conversation with the product owner and developers to explain how the software or design will be used, and finally confirm the acceptance criteria against the definition of done. Creating the card is the essential first step.

question: You're working on the product backlog with your agile project team and creating user stories to be prioritized. Which of the below is the best answer for what should go into a user story card?

options.A: Key risks of the project and the feature being developed with their owners and mitigations
options.B: A wireframe of the feature to be developed that developers can work with
options.C: The requirement, its criticality, expected development and test duration, and the acceptance criteria for that story
options.D: The benefit of the feature so that key stakeholders are aware of what they'll get

explanation: Agile story cards should have the requirement, criticality, test details and clear acceptance criteria; this gives the team everything needed to develop, test and confirm completion of the story against the definition of done. While wireframes and benefit statements are useful, the core mandatory content is the requirement, its priority, estimates and acceptance criteria.

question: You're an agile project manager and have begun creating user stories with your agile project team to go into the product backlog. You have created the card and added requirements, criticality and acceptance criteria. What are you going to do next?

options.A: Place the card on the kanban board so that the team can clearly see the future work
options.B: Have a conversation with the developers about how the software will be used and confirm the acceptance criteria and the definition of done
options.C: Prioritize the card in the upcoming sprint backlog to ensure the work gets done
options.D: Advise the project sponsor and risk manager of the feature details to ensure risks are captured

explanation: Following the three C's of user story creation (Card, Conversation, Confirmation), after creating the card the next step is to have a conversation with the developers about how the software will be used, and then confirm the acceptance criteria and definition of done before placing it in the backlog. Prioritization and placement on the kanban board come after the card is fully fleshed out.
